proposals.title: Spatially-Annotated Single-Cell Profiling Links Hypoxia-Induced NOS1 Activity to Regional TIL Dysfunction in Human Solid Tumors
proposals.motivation: Tumor hypoxia is a major barrier to immunotherapy, but its spatially heterogeneous impact on the anti-tumor T-cell repertoire is poorly understood. Pre-2026 work shows that nitric oxide synthase 1 (NOS1) is upregulated in hypoxic tumor cells and can suppress T-cell function, yet a direct, spatially-resolved analysis of NOS1-mediated immunosuppression in patient tumors is lacking. This gap prevents the rational targeting of hypoxia-driven resistance mechanisms to improve adoptive T-cell therapies like TIL.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that intratumoral hypoxic niches are enriched for tumor cell-derived NOS1, which creates geographically constrained 'immune deserts' by selectively impairing the metabolic fitness and interferon-gamma response of neoantigen-reactive CD8+ T cells.
proposals.method: We will integrate clinical FMISO-PET imaging with multi-region sampling of metastatic lesions from patients (e.g., melanoma, cholangiocarcinoma) undergoing TIL therapy. From spatially annotated tumor digests, we will perform single-cell RNA sequencing (10x Genomics) paired with surface capture of neoantigen-reactive T-cell receptors (TCRs) using patient-specific pMHC tetramers. Bioinformatic analysis will use Seurat for clustering and differential expression, spatial correlation of hypoxia signatures with NOS1 expression and T-cell dysfunction scores, and trajectory inference (Monocle3) to model T-cell exhaustion within hypoxic regions. Functional validation will use patient-derived tumor organoids co-cultured with autologous TILs under hypoxic conditions with NOS1 inhibition.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first high-resolution map linking a specific enzymatic immunosuppressant (NOS1) to geographic T-cell dysfunction in human tumors. It will identify hypoxia-specific biomarkers of TIL failure and directly test NOS1 inhibition as a strategy to 'precondition' resistant tumor niches for enhanced T-cell therapy efficacy.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct spatial correlation of clinical hypoxia imaging with single-cell functional genomics of captured neoantigen-reactive T cells, moving beyond bulk analyses to pinpoint mechanism-specific dysfunction within therapeutically relevant microenvironments.

proposals.title: Causal Validation of NOS1 as a Pan-Cancer Immune Evasion Hub via Multi-Omic Profiling and Patient-Derived Model Perturbation
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 analyses of The Cancer Genome Atlas (TCGA) reveal that amplification of NOS1, encoding neuronal nitric oxide synthase, correlates with poor survival and immune-excluded tumor microenvironments across multiple cancers. However, these associations are correlative, and the causal mechanisms by which tumor-intrinsic NOS1 drives T-cell dysfunction and resistance to immune checkpoint blockade remain undefined. Establishing NOS1 as a bona fide, actionable immune evasion hub is critical for developing novel combination therapies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that tumor- and myeloid-compartment-specific NOS1 overexpression is sufficient to suppress neoantigen-reactive T-cell responses by inducing a state of functional exhaustion and metabolic inhibition, thereby driving primary resistance to immunotherapy.
proposals.method: We will first create a pan-cancer atlas by integrating TCGA genomic (NOS1 amplification), transcriptomic (immune dysfunction signatures), and clinical data. We will validate spatial relationships using single-cell RNA-seq and CITE-seq on primary tumor digests from patients, mapping NOS1 expression to malignant and myeloid subsets. Causality will be tested by overexpressing NOS1 in genetically engineered mouse models and in patient-derived tumor organoid/autologous T-cell co-cultures, where we will quantify neoantigen-reactive TCR expansion and function using established activation-induced marker and cytokine secretion assays.
proposals.contribution: This work will move the field from correlation to causation by defining the cellular mechanisms of NOS1-mediated immunosuppression. It will deliver a validated, mechanistic model identifying NOS1 as a targetable signaling node to restore T-cell function, directly informing the design of NOS1-inhibitor and immunotherapy combination trials.
proposals.novelty: While NOS1's correlation with poor prognosis is known, this project uniquely employs patient-derived organoid/immune co-cultures and compartment-specific genetic perturbations to establish direct, causal links between NOS1 activity, T-cell dysfunction, and immunotherapy resistance.

proposals.title: Patient-Specific Biomaterial Scaffolds Engineered with NOS1-Inhibitory Cues Rescue and Expand Functional Tumor-Infiltrating Lymphocytes from Immunosuppressive Cancers
proposals.motivation: Adoptive cell therapy using tumor-infiltrating lymphocytes (TILs) has shown efficacy in melanoma but fails in many epithelial cancers due to poor ex vivo expansion of functional T-cells, linked to immunosuppressive tumor microenvironments. Pre-2026 pan-cancer analyses identify nitric oxide synthase 1 (NOS1) as a key mediator of T-cell dysfunction and exhaustion. Current TIL expansion methods using generic cytokine cocktails do not counteract these patient-specific immunosuppressive pathways, limiting the therapy's applicability.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that engineering 3D biomaterial scaffolds with patient-tailored biochemical cues—specifically targeting the NOS1-mediated dysfunction signature identified in the donor tumor—will create an ex vivo microenvironment that rescues and expands functional, neoantigen-reactive TILs from cancers historically refractory to TIL therapy.
proposals.method: We will fabricate porous, collagen-based 3D scaffolds functionalized with patient-specific cues. First, we will profile NOS1 expression and related immune dysfunction signatures (e.g., exhaustion markers, metabolic profiles) from patient tumor organoids or digests. Based on this profile, scaffolds will be loaded with precise combinations of localized NOS1 inhibitors (like L-NMMA), metabolic modulators (e.g., antioxidants), and immobilized cytokine gradients (IL-2, IL-15). TILs from metastatic digests, particularly from cancers like cholangiocarcinoma or pancreatic adenocarcinoma, will be cultured in these tailored scaffolds. T-cell functionality will be assessed via autologous tumor organoid co-cultures, TCR sequencing for clonality, and neoantigen-reactive TCR capture assays.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel, precision manufacturing platform for TIL therapy, directly translating a systemic immune biomarker into a therapeutic production strategy. It will provide proof-of-concept that counteracting defined, patient-specific immunosuppressive mechanisms ex vivo can unlock TIL therapy for a broader range of solid tumors. The resulting biomaterial design principles could be generalized to other resistance pathways beyond NOS1.
proposals.novelty: While biomaterials have been used for T-cell expansion, this approach is novel in its direct integration of a pan-cancer immune atlas to inform patient-specific scaffold engineering, moving beyond generic expansion to actively reverse a defined transcriptional and metabolic dysfunction program.

proposals.title: Functional and Repertoire Analysis of Subdominant Neoantigen-Reactive T Cells for Broadened Anti-Tumor Immunity
proposals.motivation: Current neoantigen-targeted immunotherapies, including adoptive cell transfer (ACT), often focus on immunodominant epitopes, which can lead to narrow T-cell receptor (TCR) repertoires and immune escape. Pre-2026 evidence from chronic viral infections suggests that targeting subdominant epitopes can promote broader, more durable immunity, a paradigm largely untested in human cancer. This project addresses the critical need to design therapies that prevent tumor immune evasion.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that subdominant neoantigen-reactive T cells, though less frequent in the tumor microenvironment, possess TCRs with distinct functional properties and can elicit a broader, more effective anti-tumor response compared to T cells targeting a single immunodominant epitope.
proposals.method: We will use patient-derived tumor organoids and autologous immune cells from metastatic epithelial cancers to profile T-cell responses against a panel of validated neoantigens. Using our established method for cell surface marker-based capture (CD137/OX40) of neoantigen-reactive CD8+ T cells from tumor digests, we will isolate and clone TCRs specific for both immunodominant and subdominant epitopes. The functional avidity, cytokine polyfunctionality, and in vitro tumor-killing capacity of these TCRs will be compared. TCR sequencing will be performed to analyze clonality and diversity.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct functional comparison of dominant versus subdominant neoantigen-reactive TCRs in human cancer, challenging the sufficiency of targeting single immunodominant epitopes. It will generate a framework for selecting TCRs for ACT based on epitope hierarchy and functional breadth, potentially leading to more resilient therapeutic designs.
proposals.novelty: While Rosenberg's prior work identified reactive TCRs, it did not systematically interrogate the epitope hierarchy within a patient's neoantigen landscape. This proposal uniquely applies viral immunology concepts of immunodominance to the personalized analysis of human tumor-infiltrating lymphocytes, linking epitope status to TCR function and therapeutic potential.

proposals.title: Engineering Anchor-Modified Neoantigen Peptides for HLA-A1, HLA-A3, and HLA-B7 to Expand Patient Eligibility for T-Cell Receptor Therapy
proposals.motivation: Personalized T-cell receptor (TCR) therapy targeting neoantigens is a promising cancer treatment, but its application is often limited to patients with the common HLA-A*02:01 allele. This excludes a significant portion of the patient population with other prevalent alleles like HLA-A1, A3, and B7. Pre-2026 work from our group and others has successfully used anchor-modified peptides to stabilize HLA-A2 binding and enrich neoantigen-reactive T cells, but a systematic approach for other alleles is lacking.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that computational design of anchor residue-modified peptides for high-frequency, non-A2 HLA alleles (A1, A3, B7) will enable the efficient capture and expansion of neoantigen-reactive T cells from patients harboring these alleles, broadening the applicability of personalized TCR therapies.
proposals.method: We will leverage pre-2026 HLA-peptide binding prediction algorithms (NetMHCpan-4.0, NetMHCstabpan) to identify candidate anchor modifications for patient-specific neoantigens restricted to HLA-A1, A3, and B7. Using patient-derived tumor organoids and autologous peripheral blood lymphocytes from metastatic epithelial cancer patients, we will test these modified peptides in co-culture assays to stimulate and expand tumor-infiltrating lymphocytes (TILs). Reactive T-cell clones will be isolated via CD137 activation-based sorting or MHC multimer staining, and their TCRs will be sequenced and functionally validated for specificity and cytotoxicity against organoids.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a validated library of design rules for engineering peptides for non-A2 HLA alleles, directly enabling the development of TCR-based therapies for a more genetically diverse patient cohort. It will provide proof-of-concept that the modified peptide enrichment strategy, successful for HLA-A2, is generalizable, thereby increasing the potential patient population eligible for such personalized immunotherapies.
proposals.novelty: While anchor modification is established for HLA-A2, its systematic application to other high-frequency alleles using contemporary (pre-2026) prediction tools and patient-derived organoid validation models represents a novel and critical expansion of the methodology to overcome a major limitation in the field.

proposals.title: Quantitative correlation of neoantigen-reactive cytotoxic T-lymphocyte precursor frequency with clinical response in patients receiving adoptive cell therapy
proposals.motivation: Adoptive cell therapy (ACT) using tumor-infiltrating lymphocytes (TILs) or engineered T-cell receptors (TCRs) targeting neoantigens has shown promise, but clinical responses remain variable and unpredictable. A major pre-2026 challenge is the lack of robust, quantitative biomarkers to stratify patients likely to benefit. While methods exist to identify neoantigen-reactive T cells, the relationship between their pre-therapy precursor frequency and the magnitude of tumor regression is poorly defined, hindering patient selection and therapy optimization.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the pre-treatment frequency of circulating and/or tumor-infiltrating, neoantigen-reactive CD8+ T-cell clones, quantified by a high-throughput TCR capture and sequencing assay, will directly correlate with the degree of objective tumor regression following autologous TIL or TCR-engineered T-cell therapy.
proposals.method: We will employ a patient-specific, high-throughput platform combining: 1) Identification of candidate neoantigens via whole-exome and RNA sequencing of pre-treatment metastatic biopsies. 2) Capture of neoantigen-reactive CD8+ T cells using our established method of co-culturing patient PBMCs or tumor digests with autologous antigen-presenting cells loaded with neoantigen peptide pools, followed by cell surface marker (CD137/OX40) based sorting. 3) Precise quantification of reactive clonotype frequency via single-cell TCRαβ sequencing and digital droplet PCR on pre-infusion products and peripheral blood. 4) Statistical correlation of these frequencies with radiographic tumor burden changes (RECIST criteria) and duration of response using linear mixed-effects models.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel, quantitative biomarker for predicting ACT efficacy, directly linking a measurable immune parameter to clinical outcome. It will provide a framework for patient stratification, enabling the selection of individuals with high-frequency, pre-existing neoantigen-reactive clones most likely to respond. Furthermore, it will yield critical insights into the minimum reactive T-cell precursor frequency required for effective tumor control.
proposals.novelty: While prior studies identified neoantigen-reactive TCRs, this proposal uniquely focuses on establishing a rigorous, quantitative correlation between the pre-therapy *frequency* of these specific clones—measured via a robust capture and sequencing pipeline—and the continuous metric of tumor regression, moving beyond binary responder/non-responder classification.

proposals.title: Engineering Allogeneic Off-the-Shelf T Cells Expressing Patient-Specific Neoantigen-Reactive TCRs for Solid Tumor Therapy
proposals.motivation: Adoptive cell transfer using autologous tumor-infiltrating lymphocytes (TILs) or engineered T-cell receptors (TCRs) targeting patient-specific neoantigens has shown clinical efficacy in metastatic epithelial cancers. However, manufacturing patient-specific T-cell products is logistically complex, costly, and often fails for patients with lymphopenia or poor T-cell function. An off-the-shelf, allogeneic alternative could dramatically improve accessibility and scalability.
proposals.hypothesis: Healthy donor-derived T cells, engineered to express high-affinity neoantigen-reactive TCRs identified from a patient's tumor and further modified to reduce alloreactivity (e.g., via TCR/CD3 disruption), can mount effective anti-tumor responses without requiring autologous patient T cells.
proposals.method: We will leverage our established platform for isolating neoantigen-reactive TCRs from metastatic tumor digests or patient-derived tumor organoids using cell surface markers (CD137/OX40) and tetramer-based sorting. Identified TCR alpha/beta pairs will be cloned into a lentiviral vector. Concurrently, we will use CRISPR-Cas9 to disrupt the endogenous TCR alpha constant (TRAC) and beta constant (TRBC) loci, as well as CD52 (for alemtuzumab resistance), in T cells from healthy donor leukapheresis products. These engineered, allogeneic T cells will be co-cultured with autologous patient-derived tumor organoids and matched HLA-expressing antigen-presenting cells to assess neoantigen-specific cytotoxicity and cytokine release in vitro.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a proof-of-concept for a novel, scalable manufacturing pipeline that decouples neoantigen discovery from T-cell product generation. It will establish functional data on the efficacy and specificity of allogeneic TCR-engineered T cells against patient-derived tumor models, directly addressing a major translational bottleneck in personalized cancer immunotherapy.
proposals.novelty: While allogeneic CAR-T cells are being explored for shared antigens, applying this 'off-the-shelf' concept to truly personalized, patient-specific neoantigen targets by combining neoantigen-reactive TCR discovery with precise gene editing to mitigate host-vs-graft and graft-vs-host reactivities is a novel and high-risk/high-reward strategy.

proposals.title: Comparative functional profiling of neoantigen-reactive TCRs engineered with CD28 versus 4-1BB costimulatory domains in patient-derived tumor organoids
proposals.motivation: While CD28-based CAR-T cells show potent initial anti-tumor activity, they often exhibit terminal differentiation and poor persistence, limiting durable responses in solid tumors. In contrast, 4-1BB costimulation in CARs promotes T-cell survival and memory, but its impact on the function of engineered, patient-derived neoantigen-reactive T-cell receptors (TCRs) is poorly defined. This gap is critical as TCRs target intracellular antigens prevalent in common epithelial cancers, where persistence is paramount.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that engineering patient-derived neoantigen-reactive TCRs with a 4-1BB costimulatory domain, rather than CD28, will enhance the long-term persistence and polyfunctionality of engineered T-cells while maintaining potent cytotoxicity against autologous tumor targets.
proposals.method: We will use our established platform to isolate neoantigen-reactive CD8+ T-cells and their paired TCRs from metastatic tumor digests of patients with common epithelial cancers (e.g., colorectal, breast). For each validated TCR, we will construct second-generation TCR-ζ vectors incorporating either a CD28 or 4-1BB intracellular signaling domain. These vectors will be used to engineer primary human T-cells from the same donor. Functional profiling will be performed in co-cultures with matched patient-derived tumor organoids, assessing cytotoxicity, cytokine polyfunctionality (IFN-γ, TNF-α, IL-2), and longitudinal persistence via serial re-challenge assays over 14 days. Phenotypic markers of exhaustion (PD-1, TIM-3, LAG-3) and memory (CD62L, CD127) will be tracked by flow cytometry.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct, head-to-head comparison of costimulatory domains for patient-derived neoantigen-reactive TCRs in a clinically relevant autologous system. It will yield a functional blueprint for optimizing next-generation TCR-engineered T-cell therapies by linking specific costimulatory signals to critical qualities like persistence and stemness. The findings will directly inform the design of more effective and durable personalized T-cell treatments for solid tumors.
proposals.novelty: While costimulatory domains have been compared in synthetic CAR systems, this study uniquely applies this critical engineering question to naturally occurring, patient- and neoantigen-specific TCRs, assessed in their authentic tumor microenvironment using patient-derived organoids. This moves beyond model antigens to a personalized therapeutic context.

proposals.title: Defining the Minimum Effective Dose of Neoantigen-Reactive TILs through Ex Vivo Fitness Modulation for Metastatic Solid Tumors
proposals.motivation: Adoptive cell transfer (ACT) of tumor-infiltrating lymphocytes (TILs) or engineered T cells for solid tumors often requires high cell doses, which correlate with severe toxicity like cytokine release syndrome. This toxicity limits treatment accessibility and safety. A pre-2026 challenge is to uncouple efficacy from sheer cell number by enhancing the intrinsic quality and persistence of the infused T-cell product.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that ex vivo modulation of neoantigen-reactive TILs to enhance metabolic and stem-like fitness will enable sustained antitumor activity at significantly lower cell doses, achieving equivalent tumor control while markedly reducing treatment-related toxicity.
proposals.method: We will isolate neoantigen-reactive CD8+ T cells from metastatic tumor digests of epithelial cancer patients using our established cell surface marker (CD39/OX40) capture method. Reactive populations will be expanded under two conditions: standard rapid expansion protocol (REP) versus a fitness-promoting protocol using interleukin-7 (IL-7), interleukin-15 (IL-15), and a glycolysis inhibitor (2-DG). We will assess fitness via mitochondrial mass (MitoTracker), ROS levels, and transcription factor (TCF1/7) expression. Dose-response efficacy and toxicity will be evaluated in patient-derived tumor organoid co-cultures and in vivo using humanized mouse models, comparing low-dose fitness-enhanced TILs to high-dose standard TILs.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a framework for 'dose de-escalation' in TIL therapy by defining a minimum effective dose based on cellular fitness metrics. It will directly translate to safer ACT protocols by reducing the manufacturing burden and the risk of severe adverse events, broadening the therapeutic window for solid cancer immunotherapy.
proposals.novelty: While others have explored fitness modulation, applying this specifically to *patient-derived, neoantigen-reactive* TILs to enable a deliberate low-dose strategy is novel. This shifts the paradigm from maximizing cell number to optimizing cell quality for dose reduction.

proposals.title: A Phase 3 Randomized Trial of CD19-Specific CAR T-Cell Therapy Versus Allogeneic Hematopoietic Stem Cell Transplantation for Pediatric B-Cell Acute Lymphoblastic Leukemia in Second or Later Complete Remission
proposals.motivation: For children with relapsed/refractory B-ALL, both CD19-CAR T-cells (e.g., tisagenlecleucel) and allogeneic HSCT are potentially curative but carry distinct toxicity profiles and long-term implications. As of 2025, no randomized data exists to guide the choice between these two intensive cellular therapies for patients achieving a subsequent remission, leading to variable institutional practices. A direct comparison is critical to define the optimal treatment pathway, balancing efficacy, toxicity, and quality of life.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that CD19-CAR T-cell therapy will result in superior event-free survival with a more favorable toxicity profile, particularly lower rates of chronic graft-versus-host disease, compared to allogeneic HSCT in pediatric patients with B-ALL in second or later complete remission.
proposals.method: This is a global, multi-center, open-label, phase 3 randomized controlled trial. Patients aged 1-21 years with B-ALL in second or later morphological complete remission (MRD-negative or positive) will be randomized 1:1 to receive either commercial CD19-CAR T-cell infusion or proceed to a myeloablative allogeneic HSCT from a matched donor. The primary endpoint is 3-year event-free survival. Key secondary endpoints include overall survival, incidence of severe cytokine release syndrome and neurotoxicity, incidence and severity of acute and chronic GVHD, health-related quality of life metrics, and comparative cost-effectiveness. Stratification factors include remission number and MRD status at randomization.
proposals.contribution: This trial will provide the first high-level evidence to guide clinical decision-making between two standard but fundamentally different cellular therapies. It will establish whether CAR-T can replace transplant in this setting, potentially sparing patients the long-term burdens of GVHD and immunosuppression. The correlative studies will also yield critical biological data on immune reconstitution and persistence in both arms.
proposals.novelty: While both therapies are individually established, their direct, randomized comparison in a pediatric population is novel and addresses a major unresolved question in clinical oncology. The trial design leverages the recent standardization and commercial availability of CAR-T products (pre-2026) to enable a pragmatic, definitive comparison against the historical transplant standard.

proposals.title: A Phase I/II Study Correlating Lymphodepletion Intensity with CAR-T Cell Clonal Dynamics and Persistence in Metastatic Solid Tumors
proposals.motivation: Despite the success of CAR-T therapy in hematologic malignancies, its efficacy in solid tumors remains limited, partly due to poor CAR-T cell expansion and persistence post-infusion. Pre-2026, lymphodepletion with fludarabine and cyclophosphamide (Flu/Cy) is standard to enhance engraftment, but the optimal intensity for solid tumors is undefined, with regimens varying widely. This creates a critical gap in designing preconditioning protocols to maximize the therapeutic window for adoptive cell transfer in epithelial cancers.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a moderate-intensity Flu/Cy lymphodepletion regimen will optimally deplete immunosuppressive regulatory T cells and create cytokine niches, leading to superior CAR-T cell clonal expansion and long-term persistence compared to low or high-intensity regimens in patients with metastatic epithelial cancers.
proposals.method: We will conduct a multi-cohort Phase I/II trial in patients with FGFR2-fusion positive cholangiocarcinoma and other common epithelial cancers, administering anti-FGFR2 or neoantigen-targeting CAR-T cells following one of three Flu/Cy dose levels (low, moderate, high). Using patient-derived tumor organoids and peripheral blood samples, we will longitudinally track CAR-T clonal dynamics via single-cell TCR sequencing (scTCR-seq) of the engineered product and tumor-infiltrating lymphocytes. Persistence and functional reactivity will be quantified using flow cytometry-based capture assays for activation markers and co-culture with autologous organoids.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first patient-derived, quantitative map linking lymphodepletion intensity to CAR-T clonal expansion and durability in solid tumors. It will establish a biomarker-defined, optimized Flu/Cy regimen for future trials. The study will also extend Dr. Rosenberg's expertise in neoantigen-reactive TCR capture to the engineered CAR-T setting, informing the design of more persistent cellular therapies.
proposals.novelty: While lymphodepletion is routinely used, this study systematically ties dose intensity to deep, single-cell resolution CAR-T clonal tracking and patient-specific organoid reactivity assays, moving beyond peripheral blood counts to mechanistic immunodynamics. It applies rigorous T-cell analysis methods, previously used for tumor-infiltrating lymphocytes, directly to the optimization of CAR-T manufacturing and administration protocols.

proposals.title: Chimeric Antigen Receptor-Engineered Regulatory T Cells Targeting a Citrullinated Vimentin Neoantigen for the Suppression of Rheumatoid Arthritis-Associated Autoreactive T Cells
proposals.motivation: Autoimmune diseases like rheumatoid arthritis (RA) are driven by pathogenic, autoreactive T cells that recognize self-antigens, such as citrullinated peptides. Current immunosuppressive therapies are broad and non-specific, leading to systemic side effects. The success of CAR-T cells in oncology demonstrates the power of engineered specificity, but applying this precision to suppress, rather than activate, immune responses in autoimmunity remains a critical unmet need.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that CAR-engineered regulatory T cells (CAR-Tregs) targeting a disease-specific, post-translationally modified neoantigen (citrullinated vimentin) can be generated from patient samples and will selectively suppress the activation and effector functions of autoreactive T cells in an antigen-specific manner.
proposals.method: We will isolate T cells and generate patient-derived tumor organoids from synovial tissue of RA patients. Using our established methods for capturing neoantigen-reactive TCRs, we will identify and clone TCRs specific for citrullinated vimentin peptides presented on relevant HLA. These TCR-derived scFvs will be used to construct a second-generation CAR (CD28/CD3ζ) and transduced into autologous CD4+CD25+CD127lo Tregs. The suppressive function of these CAR-Tregs will be tested in co-culture with autologous citrullinated vimentin-reactive T cells and patient-derived organoids, measuring cytokine secretion and T cell proliferation.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a proof-of-concept platform for engineering antigen-specific Tregs for autoimmune disease. It will demonstrate the feasibility of applying neoantigen-discovery pipelines from oncology to identify autoimmune targets and generate a novel, potent, and specific cellular therapeutic candidate for RA.
proposals.novelty: This inverts the core principle of Rosenberg's neoantigen-reactive T cell work—from activating cytotoxic responses against cancer to suppressing pathogenic responses in autoimmunity. It uniquely combines the identification of disease-specific modified self-antigens with the precision of CAR engineering in the suppressive Treg lineage, a synthesis not yet achieved pre-2026.

proposals.title: Adoptive Transfer of Alloreactive TCR-Engineered T Cells to Break Tumor Immune Tolerance in Epithelial Cancers
proposals.motivation: Despite advances in identifying patient-specific neoantigen-reactive T cells, solid tumors remain resistant due to profound local and systemic immune tolerance. In contrast, alloreactive T cells from transplant rejection are inherently capable of overcoming HLA-matched recipient tolerance. Leveraging this powerful biology could provide a direct route to shatter the immunosuppressive tumor microenvironment in common epithelial cancers where current adoptive cell therapies show limited efficacy.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that T cell receptors (TCRs) cloned from alloreactive T cells, when engineered into patient-derived T cells, will recognize HLA-presented tumor neoantigens with high affinity and mediate superior anti-tumor activity by bypassing central and peripheral tolerance mechanisms that constrain endogenous tumor-infiltrating lymphocytes.
proposals.method: We will isolate alloreactive T cells from mixed lymphocyte reactions (MLR) between healthy donor PBMCs and patient-derived HLA-matched tumor organoids from colorectal and pancreatic cancers. Using our established method for capturing neoantigen-reactive TCRs via cell surface markers (CD137/OX40), we will sequence paired TCRα/β chains from reactive clones. These TCRs will be cloned and transduced into autologous patient T cells. Their functional avidity and specificity will be tested against organoids and peptide pools, comparing them to TCRs derived directly from tumor digests using cytotoxicity, cytokine multiplex, and high-dimensional spectral flow cytometry.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel platform for sourcing high-avidity, tolerance-breaking TCRs for adoptive therapy. It will provide direct functional evidence that alloreactivity can be harnessed against private tumor neoantigens, potentially expanding the TCR repertoire available for treatment beyond the limited pool found within the tumor. The findings could redefine strategies for engineering T cells to overcome the suppressive tumor milieu.
proposals.novelty: While alloreactivity is a well-known barrier in transplantation, its deliberate exploitation as a source of TCRs for cancer immunotherapy is novel. This approach fundamentally differs from using tumor-infiltrating or vaccine-induced TCRs, as it seeks TCRs never subjected to tumor-driven tolerance selection.

proposals.title: Prophylactic vaccination with patient-derived neoantigen-reactive TCRs for Lynch syndrome carriers: a pre-clinical feasibility study using organoid models
proposals.motivation: Therapeutic cancer vaccines face a hostile, immunosuppressive tumor microenvironment, limiting their efficacy. A prophylactic approach in high-risk, pre-cancerous individuals, such as carriers of Lynch syndrome (LS) with germline mismatch repair mutations, could prime a potent T-cell response before immune evasion mechanisms are established. This paradigm shift from treatment to prevention could intercept cancer at its earliest, most vulnerable stage.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that TCRs specific for shared neoantigens derived from common driver mutations in LS-associated tumors can be identified from therapeutic settings and will elicit robust, protective T-cell responses when used in a prophylactic vaccine model, preventing or delaying tumorigenesis in high-risk tissues.
proposals.method: We will isolate tumor-infiltrating lymphocytes (TILs) from resected colorectal and endometrial cancers of LS patients, using the cell surface marker-based capture (CD137/OX40) to enrich neoantigen-reactive CD8+ T cells. Reactive TCRs will be sequenced and cloned. Patient-derived colonic organoids from LS carriers will be engineered via CRISPR-Cas9 to express the target neoantigens (e.g., frameshift peptides from TGFBR2, ACVR2A). These organoids will be used as antigen-presenting targets in co-cultures with TCR-transduced autologous peripheral T cells to assess preventive cytolytic activity in vitro.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel pipeline for translating therapeutic TCRs into a prophylactic context. It will provide the first pre-clinical evidence for the feasibility of personalized, TCR-based vaccines for cancer prevention in a genetically defined high-risk population, moving the field beyond shared tumor-associated antigens.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in inverting the established Rosenberg lab paradigm—using patient-derived, therapeutically validated neoantigen-reactive TCRs not for treating established tumors, but for preventing cancer in a pre-malignant, immunocompetent setting modeled by genetically engineered patient organoids.

proposals.title: Adoptive Transfer of Neoantigen-Reactive TILs for the Treatment of Minimal Residual Disease Following Cytoreductive Surgery in Metastatic Colorectal Cancer
proposals.motivation: Despite advances in immunotherapy, bulky, immunosuppressive tumors often resist T-cell infiltration and function. Cytoreductive surgery can eliminate the bulk of disease, but patients inevitably relapse from minimal residual disease (MRD), which remains a major cause of mortality. The post-surgical MRD state, characterized by a lower tumor burden and potentially a less immunosuppressive microenvironment, may represent a critical window of opportunity for effective adoptive cell therapy.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that adoptive transfer of autologous, ex vivo-expanded tumor-infiltrating lymphocytes (TILs) specifically reactive to patient-specific neoantigens will be more effective at eradicating MRD and preventing relapse than the same therapy applied to bulky metastatic disease, due to enhanced T-cell engraftment, persistence, and function in a less immunosuppressive context.
proposals.method: We will treat patients with metastatic microsatellite stable colorectal cancer. After surgical debulking, residual lesions will be biopsied to generate patient-derived tumor organoids (PDTOs). Using our established method of cell surface marker-based capture (CD39+CD103+ for CD8+ TILs), we will isolate neoantigen-reactive T-cell receptors (TCRs) from both the primary bulky tumor and the MRD biopsy. Autologous TILs will be expanded ex vivo and infused post-operatively. We will compare the clonal persistence, functional avidity, and transcriptional profiles of TILs targeting shared neoantigens between bulky and MRD contexts using single-cell RNA/TCR sequencing.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct clinical and immunological comparison of neoantigen-targeting TIL therapy in MRD versus bulky disease settings. It will define whether the MRD microenvironment is more permissive for T-cell activity and establish a novel treatment paradigm integrating cytoreduction with personalized adoptive T-cell transfer. The study will yield critical biomarkers for TIL efficacy in the post-surgical setting.
proposals.novelty: While Rosenberg's lab has pioneered methods to identify neoantigen-reactive TCRs from bulky tumors, applying these highly personalized therapies specifically in the MRD context—a distinct biological and immunological state—is a novel and logical next step. This proposal shifts the focus from treating established, refractory disease to preventing recurrence, a major unmet need.

proposals.title: Direct in vivo activation of tumor-resident lymphocytes via intratumoral cytokine/checkpoint blockade negates the need for ex vivo LAK expansion in metastatic epithelial cancers
proposals.motivation: Adoptive cell therapies like ex vivo-expanded Lymphokine-Activated Killer (LAK) cells are costly, logistically complex, and suffer from poor persistence in vivo. In vivo cytokine stimulation (e.g., IL-2) or checkpoint blockade (e.g., anti-PD-1) can activate endogenous tumor-infiltrating lymphocytes (TILs), but it is unknown if this can functionally replace ex vivo LAK expansion for generating potent, neoantigen-reactive T-cell responses in patients with common metastatic epithelial cancers.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that direct in vivo modulation of the tumor microenvironment via intratumoral delivery of IL-2 and anti-PD-1 will activate and expand a polyclonal repertoire of neoantigen-reactive T cells to a level comparable to or greater than that achieved by ex vivo LAK expansion from the same patient's tumor.
proposals.method: We will utilize patient-derived tumor organoids and autologous peripheral blood mononuclear cells (PBMCs) from metastatic colorectal and pancreatic cancer patients. For each patient, we will compare three conditions: 1) ex vivo LAK expansion from tumor digests, 2) in vivo-mimetic culture of tumor fragments with intratumoral cytokines (IL-2, IL-15) and anti-PD-1, and 3) a control condition. Reactive T-cell populations from each condition will be isolated using our established cell surface marker-based capture method (CD137/OX40 for CD8+, CD134/CD25 for CD4+). The specificity, clonality (via TCRβ sequencing), and functional avidity of these T cells will be assessed against autologous organoids and defined neoantigen peptide libraries.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a direct, quantitative comparison of the neoantigen-reactive T-cell repertoires generated by standard ex vivo expansion versus a simplified in vivo stimulation strategy. It will establish whether in vivo cytokine/checkpoint therapy can be a viable, cost-effective alternative to complex cellular manufacturing, potentially expanding access to personalized T-cell therapies.
proposals.novelty: While in vivo immunotherapies and ex vivo TIL expansions are both established, a head-to-head functional and clonal comparison of the resulting neoantigen-reactive T-cell repertoires within the same patient's tumor system has not been performed, making the potential to obviate ex vivo expansion non-obvious.

proposals.title: Integrating Tumor Histology, Immune Phenotype, and Neoantigen Landscape to Predict Metastatic Cancer Responsiveness to Lymphokine-Activated Killer (LAK) Cell Therapy
proposals.motivation: Despite historical use, LAK cell therapy has shown variable and often limited efficacy in solid tumors, leading to its decline in favor of more targeted immunotherapies like TCR-engineered T-cells. However, LAK therapy retains potential as an 'off-the-shelf' cellular product for patients lacking identifiable neoantigen-reactive T-cells. A major pre-2026 knowledge gap is the absence of biomarkers to stratify patients whose tumor microenvironment (TME) and intrinsic tumor biology are permissive to LAK-mediated killing.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that metastatic cancers with specific histologic features (e.g., poor lymphocyte infiltration) and a concurrent immune phenotype characterized by high NKG2D ligand expression and low MHC-I will demonstrate superior ex vivo and clinical responsiveness to autologous LAK cell therapy.
proposals.method: We will leverage patient-derived tumor organoids (PDTOs) and matched tumor digests from a diverse cohort of metastatic epithelial cancers (e.g., cholangiocarcinoma, colorectal, ovarian). Using pre-2026 multi-parameter flow cytometry and spatial transcriptomics (e.g., GeoMx DSP), we will profile baseline TME features (CD8+ T-cell density, macrophage polarization) and tumor cell surface markers (MICA/B, ULBP1-6, HLA-ABC). PDTOs will be co-cultured with autologous LAK cells expanded from peripheral blood, and cytotoxicity will be quantified via live-cell imaging and IFN-γ ELISA. A Random Forest regression model will integrate histology, immune phenotype, and LAK cytotoxicity data to generate a predictive responsiveness signature.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the first integrated biomarker framework for patient selection in LAK therapy, moving it from an empirical to a precision modality. It will directly inform the design of future clinical trials by identifying tumor types and immune contexts where this less personalized, more broadly applicable cellular therapy could be most effective, particularly for patients with 'cold' tumors resistant to other immunotherapies.
proposals.novelty: While Dr. Rosenberg's lab has excelled at identifying neoantigen-reactive TCRs, this proposal applies similar patient-specific analytic rigor to a non-antigen-specific, innate immune therapy, seeking to define its niche. The integration of PDTO-based functional killing assays with deep immune phenotyping to predict LAK response is a novel, mechanism-driven approach to resurrect and refine an older cellular therapy.

proposals.title: Engineering Patient-Specific Neoantigen-Reactive TCRs onto Polyclonal T Cells for Enhanced Tumor Recognition and Killing
proposals.motivation: Adoptive cell transfer (ACT) of tumor-infiltrating lymphocytes (TILs) shows efficacy but is limited by the difficulty of reliably expanding rare, high-avidity neoantigen-reactive T cells from each patient's tumor. Conversely, non-specific lymphokine-activated killer (LAK) cell therapies lack tumor specificity and cause toxicity. A method to rapidly confer patient-specific tumor recognition onto a scalable lymphocyte population is needed to improve the potency and applicability of ACT.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that transferring patient-derived, neoantigen-reactive T-cell receptors (TCRs), identified via our cell-surface marker capture method, into polyclonal peripheral blood T cells will generate a potent, tumor-specific cellular product that outperforms conventional LAK cells in recognizing and killing autologous patient tumor organoids.
proposals.method: We will isolate neoantigen-reactive CD8+ T cells from metastatic tumor digests of epithelial cancer patients using our established CD39/CD103-based cell-surface marker capture technique. We will perform single-cell TCR sequencing to clone the dominant reactive TCR alpha and beta chains. Using lentiviral vectors, we will engineer these patient-specific TCRs into polyclonal CD8+ T cells from the same patient's blood. The tumor-killing efficacy of these TCR-engineered T cells will be quantitatively compared to conventional LAK cells (IL-2 activated) using autologous patient-derived tumor organoids in co-culture assays, measuring IFN-γ secretion and tumor cell death.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a streamlined pipeline from neoantigen-reactive TCR discovery to the creation of a personalized, receptor-directed cellular therapy. It will provide direct pre-clinical evidence that redirecting polyclonal T cells with a patient's own tumor-specific TCRs is superior to nonspecific LAK activation, potentially defining a new ACT manufacturing paradigm.
proposals.novelty: While TCR engineering is established for shared antigens, applying it systematically to truly patient-specific neoantigens identified via functional capture, and directly benchmarking it against the historical LAK cell approach, is novel. This integrates Rosenberg lab's core strengths in neoantigen reactivity discovery and patient-derived organoid models to modernize an old therapeutic concept.

proposals.title: Defining the Optimal Transduction Threshold for Neoantigen-Reactive TCR-T Cell Therapies to Balance Potency and On-Target, Off-Tumor Toxicity
proposals.motivation: Adoptive T cell therapies using neoantigen-reactive T cell receptors (TCRs) show great promise for solid tumors, but on-target, off-tumor toxicity against normal tissues expressing low levels of target antigen remains a critical safety concern. Pre-2026 clinical experience with CAR-T cells suggests that high transduction efficiency, while maximizing potency, may also exacerbate such toxicities, but this relationship has not been systematically studied for patient-derived neoantigen-specific TCRs.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that for a given neoantigen-reactive TCR, there exists an optimal, sub-maximal percentage of TCR-transduced cells within a therapeutic product that maintains potent anti-tumor activity while significantly reducing reactivity against normal cells expressing low levels of the cognate antigen.
proposals.method: We will use patient-derived tumor organoids and autologous immune cells to isolate neoantigen-reactive CD8+ T cells and their TCRs, as per Rosenberg's established methods. For selected TCRs with known off-tumor targets, we will generate T cell products with precisely titrated transduction efficiencies (e.g., 20%, 50%, 80% TCR+). We will then co-culture these products with autologous tumor organoids and with engineered cell lines or primary organoids expressing graded levels of the target antigen to model on-target, off-tumor activity, using high-parameter flow cytometry and cytokine release to quantify potency and specificity.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, quantitative framework for manufacturing safer personalized TCR-T cell products by defining a clinically actionable 'therapeutic window' for transduction efficiency. It will deliver a practical methodology to pre-clinically de-risk on-target, off-tumor toxicity, directly informing the design of Phase I trials for metastatic epithelial cancers.
proposals.novelty: While the link between high CAR expression and toxicity is noted, applying a titrated, precision-engineered dilution of neoantigen-reactive TCRs within a polyclonal T cell product to actively modulate specificity is a novel safety strategy, moving beyond binary gene editing knockouts.

proposals.title: Defining a Predictive In Vitro Platform for On-Target, Off-Tumor Toxicity of Neoantigen-Reactive T-Cell Receptors
proposals.motivation: Adoptive T-cell therapy targeting patient-specific neoantigens is a promising strategy for solid tumors. However, as demonstrated by a fatal case of lung epithelial toxicity, strong in vitro tumor reactivity does not guarantee in vivo safety, as target antigen expression in normal tissues may be missed by standard assays. This critical safety gap hinders the clinical translation of personalized TCR therapies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a multi-tissue organoid co-culture system, incorporating patient-derived normal epithelial organoids alongside tumor organoids, will reveal TCR reactivities against physiologically expressed normal tissue antigens that are not detected by standard tumor-only in vitro assays.
proposals.method: We will use our established methods to capture neoantigen-reactive CD8+ T-cell receptors (TCRs) from metastatic tumor digests of patients with epithelial cancers (e.g., cholangiocarcinoma). TCRs with strong in vitro tumor reactivity will be transduced into donor T-cells. These TCR-T cells will be co-cultured with a panel of patient-matched, induced pluripotent stem cell (iPSC)-derived lung, liver, and gut epithelial organoids, alongside the original tumor organoids. Reactivity will be measured by multiplex cytokine secretion (IFN-γ, Granzyme B) and organoid viability assays.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel, patient-specific preclinical safety screening platform. It will provide critical data on the frequency and tissue-specificity of 'hidden' on-target, off-tumor reactivities, directly informing TCR selection for clinical trials. The findings will propose new safety criteria for personalized T-cell therapy development.
proposals.novelty: While tumor organoids are used to test neoantigen responses, systematically pairing them with iPSC-derived normal tissue organoids from the same patient for safety screening is novel. This approach moves beyond genomic expression databases to functionally test TCR reactivity against physiologically relevant normal human tissue.

proposals.title: A Phase I Dose-Escalation Study of Neoantigen-Reactive T-Cell Receptors in Patient-Derived Organoids to Establish Safety for Solid Tumor Adoptive Cell Therapy
proposals.motivation: Adoptive cell therapy with engineered T cells, including CAR-T and TCR-T, has shown remarkable efficacy in hematologic malignancies but faces significant safety challenges in solid tumors, including severe on-target/off-tumor toxicity and cytokine release syndrome. Pre-2026 clinical trials often initiate dosing at high cell numbers (≥10^10), which can lead to life-threatening adverse events before a maximum tolerated dose (MTD) is identified, particularly for novel, highly reactive TCRs targeting solid tumor neoantigens.
proposals.hypothesis: Implementing a conservative, intra-patient dose-escalation protocol starting at ultra-low cell doses (≤10^6) in a patient-specific, ex vivo organoid model will accurately predict the maximum tolerated dose and cytokine release profile, thereby establishing a safer translational framework for first-in-human trials of neoantigen-reactive TCRs.
proposals.method: We will utilize patient-derived tumor organoids and autologous immune cells from common epithelial cancers (e.g., colorectal, pancreatic). Neoantigen-reactive CD8+ T-cell clones will be identified and their TCRs sequenced using pre-2026 methods: tumor digest processing, cell surface marker-based capture (e.g., CD137/OX40), and co-culture with organoids. A defined dose-escalation algorithm (modified 3+3 design) will be applied ex vivo, starting at 10^5 TCR-transduced T cells per organoid well. Escalation will proceed through 7 half-log increments (10^5 to ~3x10^7). At each dose, we will quantify organoid killing, T-cell expansion, and multiplex cytokine release (IFN-γ, IL-2, IL-6, TNF-α, GM-CSF) to model the dose-toxicity relationship and identify the ex vivo MTD.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a robust, patient-specific preclinical platform to de-risk the transition of novel TCR therapies into clinical trials for solid tumors. It will deliver a validated dose-escalation algorithm and safety biomarker profile (cytokine signature) that can be directly translated to phase I trial design, potentially reducing severe adverse events.
proposals.novelty: While dose escalation is standard in oncology, applying a formal, ultra-low-starting-dose algorithm to a patient-derived organoid/T-cell co-culture system to preclinically define MTD for personalized neoantigen TCRs is novel. This integrates Rosenberg's expertise in neoantigen-reactive TCR capture with organoid technology to solve a critical translational safety problem.

proposals.title: Defining the Neoantigen-Reactive CAR-NK Cell Product: A Strategy to Mitigate Cytokine Release Syndrome While Preserving Anti-Tumor Efficacy
proposals.motivation: Chimeric Antigen Receptor (CAR)-T cell therapies, while potent, are frequently associated with severe and life-threatening cytokine release syndrome (CRS) and neurotoxicity, limiting their safe application. Natural Killer (NK) cells, which naturally produce a less inflammatory cytokine profile (e.g., more IFN-γ, less IL-6) upon activation, represent a promising alternative cellular platform. However, the functional and molecular profile of NK cells engineered to target patient-specific neoantigens, a core interest of our lab, remains undefined.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that primary human NK cells engineered to express CARs targeting patient-derived tumor neoantigens will exhibit potent, antigen-specific cytotoxicity while secreting a cytokine profile associated with reduced risk of CRS compared to neoantigen-reactive CAR-T cells.
proposals.method: We will leverage our established pipeline for identifying patient-specific neoantigen-reactive T-cell receptors (TCRs) from tumor digests and organoids. For selected, validated neoantigen targets (e.g., from FGFR2 fusions or mutant KRAS), we will construct second-generation CARs (anti-FGFR2 or anti-mutant pMHC) with CD3ζ and 4-1BB or 2B4 co-stimulatory domains. These CARs will be transduced into activated peripheral blood NK cells from healthy donors. We will compare in vitro cytotoxicity and cytokine secretion (multiplex Luminex for IL-6, IFN-γ, GM-CSF) of CAR-NK vs. CAR-T cells co-cultured with autologous patient-derived tumor organoids or antigen-pulsed targets.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct functional comparison of neoantigen-targeting CAR-NK and CAR-T cells, defining the safety profile (cytokine storm potential) and efficacy of a novel cellular product. It will establish a proof-of-concept for translating our lab's expertise in personalized neoantigen discovery into a potentially safer 'off-the-shelf' NK cell therapy platform.
proposals.novelty: While CAR-NK cells for shared antigens (e.g., CD19) are being explored, applying this platform to patient-specific neoantigens—combining Rosenberg lab's core competency in neoantigen reactivity with NK cell biology—is a novel strategy to personalize therapy while intrinsically improving safety.

proposals.title: Defining the Molecular Basis of Tumor Recognition by Lymphokine-Activated Killer (LAK) Cells: Distinguishing MHC-Restricted TCR Engagement from Novel Receptor-Mediated Lysis
proposals.motivation: Lymphokine-Activated Killer (LAK) cells, generated by IL-2 stimulation of peripheral blood lymphocytes, demonstrate potent cytotoxicity against a broad range of tumor targets, including MHC-I-deficient variants. This suggests a recognition mechanism distinct from classical, MHC-restricted T-cell receptors (TCRs). Elucidating this mechanism is critical for understanding a fundamental arm of anti-tumor immunity and could inform the development of novel, off-the-shelf cellular therapies that bypass tumor MHC downregulation, a common immune evasion strategy.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the primary tumor-lytic activity of human LAK cells against solid epithelial cancers is mediated by non-TCR, activating natural killer (NK)-cell receptors (e.g., NKG2D, DNAM-1) recognizing stress-induced ligands, and is not dependent on MHC-restricted, neoantigen-specific TCR recognition.
proposals.method: We will generate LAK cells from healthy donor PBMCs and patient-derived metastatic tumor digests (aligned with Rosenberg's recent methods). Using CRISPR-Cas9 gene editing, we will generate LAK cells deficient in key TCR components (CD3ζ) or NK receptors (NKG2D, DNAM-1). Cytolytic activity will be tested against a panel of patient-derived tumor organoids (including FGFR2-fusion+ cholangiocarcinoma) and their isogenic MHC-I-knockout counterparts. Single-cell RNA sequencing and surface proteomics of tumor-infiltrating LAK cells will be correlated with lytic activity to identify receptor signatures predictive of MHC-independent killing.
proposals.contribution: This work will definitively map the receptor-ligand pairs governing LAK-tumor recognition, resolving a long-standing question in tumor immunology. It will establish whether patient-derived LAK cells possess a therapeutically relevant capacity for MHC-independent tumor killing, potentially identifying a new source of effector cells for allogeneic therapy. The findings will directly inform the design of next-generation engineered immune cells.
proposals.novelty: While Rosenberg's lab has expertly isolated neoantigen-reactive TCRs, this proposal systematically investigates the alternative, non-TCR-mediated cytotoxic pathway in IL-2-activated lymphocytes from cancer patients, a mechanism presumed to exist but not rigorously dissected in the context of patient-derived tumors and organoids.

proposals.title: Development of a Closed-System Bioreactor Process for the Clinical-Scale Expansion of Lymphokine-Activated Killer (LAK) Cells from Metastatic Tumor Digests
proposals.motivation: Despite advances in neoantigen-reactive T-cell therapies, many patients lack suitable T-cell targets or have tumors with low mutational burden. Lymphokine-Activated Killer (LAK) cells, derived from tumor-infiltrating or peripheral blood lymphocytes, offer a complementary, broad-spectrum cellular immunotherapy. However, pre-2026 clinical application of LAK cells has been limited by the inability to reliably generate the large, clinically relevant cell doses (10^10-10^11 cells) required for efficacy from the limited starting material of metastatic tumor digests or patient leukapheresis.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that optimizing a GMP-compliant, closed-system bioreactor protocol with defined cytokine cocktails and perfusion feeding will enable the consistent, large-scale expansion of functionally potent LAK cells from metastatic tumor digests, achieving target cell yields while maintaining a favorable effector-to-target cell phenotype.
proposals.method: We will adapt our established methods for processing patient-derived metastatic tumor digests and capturing tumor-reactive lymphocytes. Starting cell populations (bulk tumor-infiltrating lymphocytes or CD3+ enriched fractions) will be cultured in a WAVE Bioreactor or equivalent closed, rocking-motion system. We will systematically test expansion protocols varying: 1) initial activation (anti-CD3/IL-2 vs. IL-2/IL-15 alone), 2) perfusion rate and medium exchange schedules to manage metabolite accumulation, and 3) the timing of IL-2 supplementation. Cell growth, viability, and phenotype (CD3, CD56, NKG2D, DNAM-1) will be tracked daily. The cytotoxic function of bioreactor-expanded LAK cells against autologous patient-derived tumor organoids and standard NK-sensitive targets (K562) will be compared to flask-expanded controls.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a scalable, translational manufacturing protocol for LAK cell therapy, directly addressing a major bottleneck in the field. It will provide a robust method to generate LAK cells for clinical trials in patients refractory to T-cell-targeted therapies. Furthermore, it will define critical quality attributes (expansion fold, phenotype, cytotoxicity) for clinically viable LAK cell products.
proposals.novelty: While bioreactors are used for CAR-T cells, their application for expanding LAK cells from the challenging starting material of metastatic tumor digests—a core competency of our lab—is unexplored. The novelty lies in integrating tumor digest processing, patient-derived organoid functional validation, and scalable bioprocess engineering specifically for this non-engineered, multi-effector cell product.

proposals.title: Molecular Profiling of a LAK-Resistant Metastatic Tumor to Identify Evasion Mechanisms in the Context of Patient-Specific Neoantigens
proposals.motivation: Lymphokine-activated killer (LAK) cell therapy has shown limited but variable efficacy in clinical trials, with some tumors exhibiting complete resistance. Understanding the mechanisms of this resistance is critical for improving adoptive cell therapies. This study addresses a key gap by focusing on a rare, fully resistant patient-derived tumor to define actionable evasion pathways.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the LAK-resistant tumor possesses unique molecular alterations—such as defects in death receptor signaling, upregulation of inhibitory ligands, or loss of stress-induced ligands—that shield it from LAK-mediated cytotoxicity, potentially in a manner linked to its neoantigen landscape.
proposals.method: We will use a multi-omics approach on a patient-derived LAK-resistant tumor organoid and matched sensitive controls. RNA sequencing will identify differentially expressed genes in immune evasion pathways. Surface proteomics will quantify expression of NKG2D ligands, death receptors (e.g., FAS), and checkpoint ligands (e.g., HLA-E, PD-L1). CRISPR-Cas9 screens in resistant organoids will validate candidate resistance genes by restoring LAK sensitivity. Neoantigen reactivity will be assessed using autologous tumor-infiltrating lymphocytes (TILs) expanded from the resistant tumor digest.
proposals.contribution: This work will define a specific molecular signature of LAK resistance, providing a biomarker for patient stratification. It will identify novel therapeutic targets to sensitize tumors to innate immune cell therapies. The integration with neoantigen analysis will reveal potential crosstalk between adaptive and innate immune evasion.
proposals.novelty: While most research focuses on enhancing LAK potency, this proposal uniquely applies an 'inverse problem' approach, learning from a clinical outlier of complete resistance. It integrates LAK biology with Dr. Rosenberg's established expertise in patient-derived organoids and neoantigen-reactive T-cell analysis.

proposals.title: Defining Cytokine and Co-stimulatory Signals to Sustain Neoantigen-Reactive TILs Without Preconditioning Lymphodepletion
proposals.motivation: Lymphodepletion prior to adoptive T cell transfer (ACT) is toxic and limits patient eligibility. While it creates a cytokine sink and homeostatic niche for transferred cells, its absolute necessity for T cell persistence and efficacy, particularly for neoantigen-reactive tumor-infiltrating lymphocytes (TILs), is unclear. Identifying alternative, non-toxic signals to drive expansion could broaden the applicability of ACT.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific combinations of homeostatic cytokines (e.g., IL-7, IL-15) and co-stimulatory agonists (e.g., 4-1BB, OX40) can provide sufficient expansion and survival signals for transferred neoantigen-reactive T cells, obviating the need for host lymphodepletion.
proposals.method: We will use patient-derived tumor organoids and autologous immune cells to model personalized T cell responses. Neoantigen-reactive CD8+ T cell receptors (TCRs) will be identified from metastatic digests via our established cell-surface marker (CD137/OX40) capture method. TCR-transduced peripheral blood T cells will be transferred into immunocompetent, humanized mouse models bearing matched patient-derived organoids. We will test regimens combining systemic cytokines (IL-7/IL-15) and agonist antibodies against co-stimulatory receptors, comparing T cell persistence, tumor control, and functional exhaustion to standard lymphodepletion-based ACT.
proposals.contribution: This work will define a minimal set of pharmacological signals required for effective ACT, potentially decoupling efficacy from toxic lymphodepletion. It will provide a preclinical framework for designing less toxic, next-generation ACT protocols that could be applied to a wider patient population.
proposals.novelty: While lymphodepletion is a clinical standard, systematically testing defined cytokine/co-stimulation cocktails as direct replacements in a personalized, neoantigen-reactive TCR model is novel. This moves beyond observational studies to a mechanistic, signal-based engineering of the host environment.

proposals.title: Quantitative Modeling of Lymphodepletion Intensity to Guide Minimal Conditioning for Adoptive T Cell Therapy in Epithelial Cancers
proposals.motivation: Nonmyeloablative lymphodepleting conditioning (e.g., cyclophosphamide and fludarabine) is a standard but toxic prerequisite for adoptive cell transfer (ACT) of tumor-infiltrating lymphocytes (TILs) or engineered T cells, believed to enhance engraftment by eliminating cytokine sinks and immunosuppressive cells. However, the optimal intensity is unknown, and excessive conditioning causes significant morbidity, limiting broader clinical application. Defining the minimal effective regimen is critical for improving the safety and accessibility of personalized neoantigen-reactive T cell therapies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a quantitative relationship exists between the depth of lymphodepletion (measured by absolute lymphocyte count and specific immune subset clearance) and the subsequent expansion and persistence of transferred neoantigen-reactive T cell clones, enabling the identification of a minimally toxic conditioning threshold.
proposals.method: We will leverage patient-derived tumor organoids and autologous neoantigen-reactive T cell receptors (TCRs) identified via our cell surface marker (CD137/OX40) capture platform from metastatic epithelial cancers. In a humanized mouse model, we will test ACT following a gradient of conditioning intensities. Using high-throughput TCRβ sequencing, we will track clonal kinetics of transferred cells. We will employ multivariate linear mixed-effects modeling to correlate pre-infusion immune metrics (e.g., Treg frequency, myeloid-derived suppressor cell count) and conditioning depth with peak expansion, persistence duration, and tumor control.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first data-driven framework for titrating lymphodepletion based on measurable patient-specific immune parameters. It will directly inform the design of safer, reduced-intensity conditioning protocols for clinical trials of ACT targeting neoantigens. The predictive model could transition conditioning from a fixed, toxic regimen to a personalized, optimized component of cellular immunotherapy.
proposals.novelty: While lymphodepletion is universally used, its optimization has been empirical. This study uniquely applies quantitative modeling to pre-infusion immune profiling and clonal T cell tracking to rationally define a minimal effective dose, moving beyond the paradigm of maximal tolerated lymphodepletion.

proposals.title: Engineering Synthetic AND-Gate T Cells for Tumor-Restricted Activation Using Patient-Derived Neoantigen-Reactive TCRs
proposals.motivation: Adoptive T cell therapies, including those using neoantigen-reactive T cell receptors (TCRs), can cause severe on-target, off-tumor toxicity and cytokine release syndrome due to systemic activation. Current strategies lack precise spatial control, limiting safety and efficacy. A synthetic biology approach to conditionally restrict T cell effector functions to the tumor microenvironment (TME) is a critical unmet need to improve the therapeutic window.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that engineering patient-derived neoantigen-reactive TCRs into T cells co-expressing a synthetic AND-gate circuit—requiring simultaneous TCR engagement and a TME-specific signal (e.g., hypoxia or a tumor-associated protease)—will achieve stringent, tumor-localized activation while minimizing systemic toxicity.
proposals.method: We will construct a dual-input synthetic circuit using a modular architecture. Input 1: Patient-derived, high-affinity neoantigen-reactive TCRs (identified via our established tumor digest/TIL screening platforms). Input 2: A hypoxia-responsive element (HRE) driving expression of a synthetic co-stimulatory receptor (e.g., MyD88/CD40). Primary human T cells will be transduced with lentiviral vectors encoding both components. Activation will be quantified in vitro using patient-derived tumor organoids under normoxic vs. hypoxic conditions, and in vivo using immunodeficient mouse models bearing autologous organoid-derived xenografts, measuring tumor control and systemic cytokine levels.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a generalizable platform for creating safer, next-generation adoptive T cell therapies by integrating patient-specific neoantigen targeting with synthetic biology. It will deliver a proof-of-concept for a clinically translatable logic-gated T cell product and establish functional validation assays using patient-derived organoids and TCRs.
proposals.novelty: While synthetic NOT-gates (e.g., inhibitory CARs) exist, integrating a patient's own neoantigen-reactive TCR—a highly personalized therapeutic—into a positive AND-gate circuit for tumor-restricted activation is novel. This moves beyond blanket CAR T approaches to create precision-engineered, context-dependent T cells.

proposals.title: Targeting Minimal Residual Disease with Low-Dose, Neoantigen-Reactive T Cell Receptor-Engineered T Cells Following Curative-Intent Surgery
proposals.motivation: Despite curative-intent surgery, many epithelial cancer patients relapse due to undetectable minimal residual disease (MRD) or micrometastases. Adoptive cell therapy (ACT) is typically used at high doses for bulky disease, which is logistically complex, costly, and carries toxicity risks. A scalable, low-toxicity ACT strategy is needed for the post-surgical adjuvant setting to eradicate MRD and prevent recurrence.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that low doses of autologous T cells engineered with patient-specific, high-avidity neoantigen-reactive T cell receptors (neoTCRs) can safely and effectively eliminate MRD in the post-surgical setting, where the immunosuppressive tumor microenvironment is less established.
proposals.method: We will use our established platform to identify neoantigen-reactive TCRs from tumor-infiltrating lymphocytes (TILs) or tumor digests of resected specimens from patients with colorectal or pancreatic cancer. Candidate neoTCRs will be validated for specificity and avidity using patient-derived tumor organoids. For a pilot clinical study, patients with high-risk MRD post-surgery will receive escalating micro-doses (1e5 to 1e6 cells/kg) of autologous CD8+ T cells transduced with the validated neoTCR, without high-dose lymphodepletion. Persistence and functional state of engineered cells will be tracked via TCR sequencing and flow cytometry of serial blood samples, correlating with MRD status by ctDNA.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a proof-of-concept for a miniaturized, scalable ACT paradigm for adjuvant therapy. It will generate critical data on the in vivo biology of low-dose engineered T cells in a minimal-disease setting and define a potential new clinical application for personalized neoTCR therapies.
proposals.novelty: This proposal shifts the ACT paradigm from high-intensity treatment of advanced disease to a low-dose, precision adjuvant intervention. It uniquely leverages pre-2026 advances in neoTCR discovery and organoid validation specifically for MRD targeting, a context where T cell function may be fundamentally different.

proposals.title: Adoptive Cell Therapy for Systemic Sclerosis Using Patient-Derived Neoantigen-Reactive Regulatory T Cells
proposals.motivation: Adoptive cell therapy (ACT) with tumor-infiltrating lymphocytes (TILs) has shown efficacy in metastatic cancers, but its principles have not been systematically applied to autoimmune disease. Systemic sclerosis (SSc) is a severe autoimmune condition characterized by fibrosis and vasculopathy with limited treatment options. This project aims to translate the core ACT methodology—identifying and expanding patient-specific, disease-relevant T cells—to instead target and suppress autoimmune pathology.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that regulatory T cells (Tregs) reactive to disease-specific autoantigens or neoantigens can be isolated from SSc patient lesions, expanded ex vivo, and re-infused to suppress pathogenic immune responses and ameliorate fibrosis in an antigen-specific manner.
proposals.method: We will obtain skin and lung biopsy digests from SSc patients, analogous to metastatic tumor digests. Using a pre-2026 method (e.g., CD25+CD127lo surface marker-based capture combined with activation-induced marker assays against patient-derived fibroblast or endothelial cell neoantigen libraries), we will isolate autoantigen/neoantigen-reactive Tregs. These cells will be clonally expanded using rapid expansion protocols (REP) established for TILs. Their suppressive function and antigen specificity will be validated using patient-derived dermal fibroblast organoids co-cultured with autologous effector T cells, measuring cytokine suppression and collagen production.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel, personalized ACT platform for autoimmune disease, moving beyond broad immunosuppression. It will provide proof-of-concept that the tools of cancer neoantigen discovery and T-cell receptor (TCR) isolation can be repurposed to identify disease-suppressive Treg clones. The project will generate a pipeline for manufacturing autologous, antigen-specific Tregs for clinical translation in SSc.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in applying the exact capture and expansion techniques developed for neoantigen-reactive cytotoxic T cells in cancer to the isolation of regulatory T cells for an autoimmune fibrotic disease, targeting patient-specific antigen repertoires rather than polyclonal Tregs.

proposals.title: Pre-treatment with immune checkpoint blockade expands neoantigen-reactive T-cell clones and enhances their response to high-dose IL-2 in metastatic solid tumors
proposals.motivation: High-dose interleukin-2 (IL-2) therapy can induce durable responses in some metastatic cancers, but its efficacy is limited and often considered less effective after prior immunotherapy due to presumed T-cell exhaustion. This paradigm discourages sequential treatment strategies. However, pre-2026 evidence suggests immune checkpoint inhibitors (ICIs) can expand tumor-reactive T-cell clones, potentially creating a more favorable immune context for subsequent cytokine therapy.
proposals.hypothesis: Prior treatment with immune checkpoint blockade, rather than inducing terminal exhaustion, expands a pool of tumor-infiltrating, neoantigen-reactive T cells that retain functional plasticity and exhibit enhanced proliferative and cytotoxic responses to high-dose IL-2 stimulation.
proposals.method: We will leverage patient-derived tumor organoids and autologous peripheral blood lymphocytes from melanoma and renal cell carcinoma patients treated sequentially with anti-PD-1 and then IL-2. Using our established method for cell surface marker-based capture (CD39+CD103+ for CD8+ TILs), we will isolate neoantigen-reactive T-cell receptors (TCRs) from pre- and post-ICI tumor digests. TCR sequencing and clonal tracking will quantify expansion. Functional response to IL-2 will be assessed by multiplexed cytokine secretion (IFN-γ, Granzyme B) upon co-culture with neoantigen-pulsed autologous antigen-presenting cells and by proliferation assays, comparing ICI-exposed versus ICI-naïve TCR-transduced T cells.
proposals.contribution: This work will challenge the clinical notion that prior immunotherapy is a negative predictor for IL-2, providing a mechanistic rationale for strategic sequencing of immunotherapies. It will directly link ICI-induced clonal expansion of tumor-specific T cells to enhanced responsiveness to a distinct cytokine therapy, informing patient selection and combination trial design.
proposals.novelty: While ICI-induced T-cell expansion is known, its functional consequence for subsequent, mechanistically distinct IL-2 therapy is unexplored. This proposal uniquely tests the 'priming' hypothesis by directly isolating and comparing the IL-2 responsiveness of patient-derived, neoantigen-specific TCRs pre- and post-ICI exposure.

proposals.title: Combining high-dose IL-2 with personalized neoantigen-reactive T-cell therapy to overcome resistance in metastatic epithelial cancers
proposals.motivation: High-dose interleukin-2 (HD IL-2) achieves durable responses in a subset of metastatic melanoma and renal cell carcinoma patients, but its overall response rate remains low (~16%) and its severe toxicity limits use. Concurrently, adoptive cell therapy (ACT) with tumor-infiltrating lymphocytes (TILs) or T-cell receptors (TCRs) targeting patient-specific neoantigens has shown promise, but T-cell persistence and function in the immunosuppressive tumor microenvironment remain key barriers. Combining these modalities could address these complementary limitations.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that HD IL-2 will provide critical proliferative and survival signals to adoptively transferred, neoantigen-reactive T cells, enhancing their in vivo expansion, persistence, and tumoricidal activity, thereby improving clinical responses beyond either therapy alone.
proposals.method: We will treat patients with metastatic colorectal, pancreatic, or cholangiocarcinoma. For each patient, we will generate autologous tumor organoids and use them to stimulate peripheral blood lymphocytes, isolating neoantigen-reactive CD8+ T-cell clones via cell surface marker capture (CD137/OX40). We will sequence the TCRs of reactive clones and produce large-scale TCR-transgenic T-cell products. Patients will receive lymphodepletion, followed by infusion of the TCR-T cell product and a standard HD IL-2 regimen. We will longitudinally track the transferred TCR clonotypes in blood and tumor using TCR-seq and correlate persistence with clinical response and immune profiling of serial tumor biopsies.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first clinical data on the safety and efficacy of combining HD IL-2 with personalized TCR-T therapy for common epithelial cancers. It will mechanistically define how IL-2 influences the fate of neoantigen-specific T cells in vivo, offering biomarkers for response. The work could establish a new, potentially more effective and broadly applicable paradigm for integrating cytokine therapy with precision cellular immunotherapy.
proposals.novelty: While IL-2 has been combined with checkpoint inhibitors, its synergy with ex vivo-selected, neoantigen-specific TCR-T cells—particularly for non-melanoma solid tumors—is unexplored. This approach directly tests whether providing a potent cytokine signal can overcome the functional exhaustion that often limits engineered T-cell therapies.

proposals.title: High-Dimensional Immune Profiling of Peripheral T-cell Subsets to Predict and Monitor Neoantigen-Reactive Responses to IL-2 Therapy in Metastatic Cancer
