proposals.motivation: High-dose IL-2 therapy can induce durable regression in some metastatic cancers, but its severe toxicity and low response rate limit its utility. Pre-2026, predictive biomarkers for IL-2 response are lacking. In transplant immunology, detailed flow cytometry monitoring of T-cell subsets (e.g., naive, effector, exhausted, regulatory T cells) is routine for predicting rejection or tolerance. Applying this sophisticated immune monitoring framework to IL-2 therapy could identify predictive signatures of response.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that pre-treatment peripheral blood T-cell subset profiles, particularly the frequency and phenotype of neoantigen-reactive T-cell precursors, will correlate with subsequent clinical response to IL-2, and that dynamic changes in these subsets during early treatment will track with therapeutic efficacy and toxicity.
proposals.method: We will perform high-dimensional (28+ color) flow cytometry on serial peripheral blood mononuclear cell (PBMC) samples from metastatic melanoma or renal cell carcinoma patients undergoing IL-2 therapy. Panels will include markers for differentiation (CD45RA, CCR7, CD27, CD28), exhaustion (PD-1, TIM-3, LAG-3), regulation (FoxP3, CD25), and activation (CD38, HLA-DR). We will integrate this with patient-specific neoantigen reactivity data using our established method of capturing neoantigen-reactive TCRs from tumor digests and constructing MHC multimers to track these clones in the periphery. Statistical modeling (e.g., logistic regression, longitudinal mixed-effects models) will correlate immune signatures with clinical response (RECIST) and severe toxicity.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel, clinically actionable immune monitoring framework for IL-2 therapy, moving it towards a more personalized application. It will directly link fundamental T-cell biology—specifically the dynamics of neoantigen-reactive subsets—to a critical clinical outcome. The resulting predictive signatures could be used to select patients most likely to benefit, sparing others from unnecessary toxicity.
proposals.novelty: While immune monitoring is standard in transplantation and neoantigen reactivity is studied in trial settings, the direct application of transplant-level T-cell subset analytics to predict and track IL-2 response by integrating it with patient-specific neoantigen-reactive TCR tracking is a novel, cross-disciplinary approach not yet explored.

proposals.title: Low-dose IL-2 modulates the neoantigen-reactive T-cell repertoire by preferential expansion of intratumoral regulatory T-cells
proposals.motivation: Low-dose IL-2 therapy is being explored in autoimmunity to expand regulatory T-cells (Tregs). In cancer, this could inadvertently suppress anti-tumor immunity. Pre-2026 work by Rosenberg and others has shown that tumor-infiltrating lymphocytes contain rare, potent neoantigen-reactive T-cells critical for successful immunotherapy. The impact of low-dose IL-2 on this specific, therapeutically relevant T-cell compartment within the tumor microenvironment remains unknown.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that systemic low-dose IL-2 administration in cancer patients will preferentially expand intratumoral Tregs over conventional and neoantigen-reactive CD8+ T-cells, thereby creating an immunosuppressive microenvironment that may limit adaptive T-cell therapies.
proposals.method: We will treat metastatic cancer patients with low-dose IL-2 prior to planned tumor resection. Using patient-derived tumor digests and autologous PBMCs, we will employ our established cell surface marker-based capture technology (CD134/OX40 for CD4+, CD137/4-1BB for CD8+) to isolate neoantigen-reactive T-cell receptors (TCRs) pre- and post-treatment. High-throughput TCR sequencing and single-cell RNA-seq will be performed on sorted T-cell subsets (Tregs, conventional T-cells) from tumor and blood. Functional validation of isolated TCRs will be conducted using patient-derived tumor organoids to assess changes in neoantigen recognition potency.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first direct evidence of how low-dose IL-2 shapes the functional neoantigen-specific T-cell landscape within human tumors. It will define a potential contraindication for combining low-dose IL-2 with neoantigen-directed therapies and identify TCR clonotypes most susceptible to suppression.
proposals.novelty: While low-dose IL-2's effect on peripheral Tregs is known, its specific impact on the intratumoral, neoantigen-reactive T-cell repertoire—a direct product of Rosenberg's core methodologies—is unexplored. This directly tests the 'inverse immunologic effect' in the context of human cancer immunotherapy.

proposals.title: Lentiviral Vector Engineering for Enhanced Safety and Persistence of Neoantigen-Reactive TCRs in Adoptive Cell Therapy
proposals.motivation: Adoptive cell therapy (ACT) using neoantigen-reactive T-cell receptors (TCRs) shows promise for treating metastatic cancers, but clinical efficacy is limited by inefficient and potentially genotoxic TCR gene delivery. Current retroviral systems have suboptimal transduction efficiency in primary T cells and carry risks of insertional mutagenesis. Safer, more efficient delivery systems are urgently needed to translate personalized TCR discoveries into reliable treatments.
proposals.hypothesis: Lentiviral vectors (LVs), optimized for safety in clinical gene therapy for inherited disorders, can be adapted to deliver neoantigen-reactive TCRs, resulting in higher transduction efficiency, improved T-cell persistence, and a reduced risk of insertional oncogenesis compared to conventional gamma-retroviral vectors.
proposals.method: We will engineer a self-inactivating (SIN) lentiviral vector backbone, incorporating elements from pre-2026 clinical gene therapy vectors (e.g., a chromatin insulator and a weak endogenous promoter) to minimize genotoxicity. Using TCRs previously isolated from patient tumor-infiltrating lymphocytes (TILs) targeting validated neoantigens (e.g., FGFR2 fusions), we will produce high-titer LV stocks. We will transduce autologous patient-derived peripheral blood T cells and compare transduction efficiency, TCR surface expression, and functional avidity against tumor organoids to cells transduced with a standard gamma-retroviral vector. Long-term T-cell persistence and genomic integration sites will be assessed in vitro.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a clinically translatable LV platform for personalized TCR therapy, directly addressing a key manufacturing bottleneck. It will provide critical pre-clinical data on the safety and efficacy profile of LVs for TCR delivery, potentially enabling more potent and durable T-cell products for ACT.
proposals.novelty: While LVs are used in gene therapy, their systematic application and safety engineering for delivering patient-specific, tumor-derived neoantigen-reactive TCRs—as opposed to chimeric antigen receptors (CARs) or model antigens—is novel. This bridges advanced gene therapy tools with the personalized neoantigen discovery pipeline from Rosenberg's lab.

proposals.title: High-Purity FACS Enrichment of Neoantigen-Reactive TCR-Engineered T Cells to Enhance the Potency of Adoptive Cell Therapy
proposals.motivation: Adoptive T cell therapy (ACT) using T cell receptors (TCRs) targeting patient-specific neoantigens is a promising personalized cancer treatment. A critical bottleneck is the low frequency of successfully transduced T cells in the final infusion product, which can dilute therapeutic efficacy and necessitate large-scale, costly ex vivo expansion. Current manufacturing protocols, based on bulk selection like antibiotic resistance, yield heterogeneous products with variable potency.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that using fluorescence-activated cell sorting (FACS) to specifically enrich for T cells expressing both the introduced neoantigen-reactive TCR and a favorable memory phenotype will generate a more potent and defined therapeutic product, leading to superior in vivo expansion and anti-tumor activity in patients.
proposals.method: We will isolate neoantigen-reactive TCRs from metastatic tumor digests or tumor-infiltrating lymphocytes (TILs) of epithelial cancer patients, as per our established protocols. TCRs will be cloned into retroviral vectors co-expressing a cell surface marker (e.g., truncated CD34 or NGFR). Patient T cells will be transduced and then sorted via FACS for high, uniform expression of the marker. A second sort will be applied to isolate transduced cells with a stem cell memory (Tscm) or central memory (Tcm) phenotype (CD62L+, CD45RO+, CD95+). The phenotype, polyfunctionality, and in vivo anti-tumor efficacy of FACS-enriched products will be compared to bulk-transduced products using patient-derived tumor organoid co-cultures and murine xenograft models.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a robust, clinically applicable manufacturing protocol to produce highly enriched, phenotypically defined neoantigen-reactive T cell products. It will provide direct preclinical evidence that product purity and differentiation state are critical determinants of ACT success, moving the field beyond simply quantifying transduction efficiency. The findings could be rapidly translated into ongoing clinical trials of personalized TCR therapy.
proposals.novelty: While FACS is standard in immunology research, its systematic application to enrich for both transduction and a specific memory subset in the context of personalized, patient-derived neoantigen-reactive TCRs is novel. This integrates precise cell engineering with immunological principles of T cell fitness to optimize a therapeutic product, a refinement not yet standard in ACT manufacturing.

proposals.title: Engineering the Tumor Microenvironment as a Catalytic Scaffold to Amplify Neoantigen-Reactive T-Cell Responses
proposals.motivation: Despite the identification of patient-specific, neoantigen-reactive T cells and their receptors (TCRs), adoptive T-cell therapies often fail due to the immunosuppressive and physically exclusionary tumor microenvironment (TME). The TME acts as a barrier that limits T-cell infiltration, persistence, and function. Transforming this barrier into a supportive structure could dramatically improve the efficacy of personalized immunotherapies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that targeted, synthetic modifications to the physical and biochemical properties of the TME—specifically its extracellular matrix (ECM) and stromal compartments—can convert it into an immunostimulatory 'catalytic scaffold' that actively recruits, activates, and sustains the function of infused neoantigen-reactive T cells.
proposals.method: We will utilize patient-derived tumor organoids co-cultured with autologous cancer-associated fibroblasts (CAFs) to create a 3D model of the TME. Using this model, we will test the impact of ECM-modifying enzymes (e.g., hyaluronidase, collagenase) and stromal cell re-engineering via CRISPRa to overexpress T-cell chemoattractants (CXCL9/10) and stimulatory cytokines (IL-12, IL-15). The functional outcome will be measured by quantifying the infiltration, proliferation, and tumoricidal activity of autologous neoantigen-reactive CD8+ T cells, identified and isolated using our established cell surface marker-based capture technique.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a proof-of-concept that the TME can be actively re-engineered to enhance adoptive T-cell therapy. It will provide a novel combinatorial strategy that pairs TCR-based cell therapy with microenvironmental conditioning. The findings could lead to new clinical protocols where TME-modulating agents are administered prior to or in conjunction with T-cell infusion.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in its conceptual framing of the TME as an engineerable catalytic scaffold rather than a passive barrier to be overcome. It moves beyond broad immunosuppression or depletion of stromal elements to instead actively reprogram the TME's intrinsic properties to support immunotherapy.

proposals.title: Spatially-Resolved Analysis of Radiotherapy-Induced Vascular Niche Remodeling and Neoantigen-Specific T-Cell Exclusion in Metastatic Seeding
proposals.motivation: Radiotherapy (RT) is a cornerstone of solid tumor treatment, yet local recurrence and distant metastasis remain major causes of mortality. Pre-2026 clinical and preclinical data suggest RT can paradoxically promote a pro-metastatic tumor microenvironment (TME), but the precise, causative mechanisms linking localized RT damage to enhanced tumor cell intravasation and survival are poorly defined. This gap limits our ability to design therapies that preserve RT's benefits while blocking its pro-metastatic effects.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that spatially fractionated RT creates distinct, localized niches within the primary TME characterized by vascular leakiness, activated fibroblasts, and exclusion of neoantigen-reactive T cells, which collectively enhance the intravasation and metastatic fitness of disseminated tumor cells.
proposals.method: We will employ a spatially fractionated RT technique in GEMMs of epithelial cancers (e.g., pancreatic, breast) to create irradiated and shielded sectors within the same primary tumor. Longitudinal intravital imaging will track vascular permeability and CTC release. We will perform single-cell RNA sequencing (10x Genomics) on endothelial cells, cancer-associated fibroblasts, and immune cells from laser-capture microdissected TME sectors. Crucially, we will adapt Dr. Rosenberg's established method of capturing neoantigen-reactive CD8+ T-cell receptors (TCRs) from patient tumors to instead isolate and track tumor-reactive TCRs from mouse tumor digests, using TCR sequencing to map their spatial distribution and clonal dynamics post-RT.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a causal, spatially explicit mechanism for RT-induced metastatic seeding, moving beyond correlative studies. It will identify specific RT-remodeled cellular niches and soluble factors that serve as new therapeutic targets. Furthermore, it will directly link TME remodeling to the functional exclusion of tumor-reactive T cells, providing a rationale for combining RT with tailored immunotherapies.
proposals.novelty: The integration of spatially fractionated RT with single-cell mapping of the vascular niche and in situ tracking of neoantigen-reactive T-cell clones within the same tumor is a novel approach to deconvolve pro- and anti-tumor effects of radiation. Applying patient-derived, neoantigen-focused TCR capture methods to a mechanistic GEMM study bridges translational and basic science uniquely.

proposals.title: Defining a Radiotherapy Fractionation Regimen to Reprogram the Tumor Microenvironment and Amplify Neoantigen-Specific T Cell Responses in Metastatic Cancers
proposals.motivation: Despite advances in adoptive T cell therapy targeting neoantigens, durable responses in common epithelial cancers remain limited by the immunosuppressive tumor microenvironment (TME). Radiotherapy can modulate the TME, but standard fractionation is optimized for direct tumor cell kill, not for creating a pro-inflammatory, immune-permissive niche. A rational, multi-compartment approach to radiotherapy dosing is needed to convert the TME from a barrier into an asset for cellular immunotherapy.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a specific, moderate-dose-per-fraction radiotherapy schedule exists that can simultaneously induce immunogenic tumor cell death, reprogram immunosuppressive stromal cells, and preserve endothelial function, thereby enhancing the expansion and function of adoptively transferred neoantigen-reactive T cells.
proposals.method: We will use patient-derived tumor organoids (PDTOs) co-cultured with autologous stromal and immune cells to model the TME. PDTOs from metastatic lesions (e.g., cholangiocarcinoma, colorectal) with defined neoantigens or fusion proteins (e.g., FGFR2) will be irradiated with varying dose-per-fraction schedules (e.g., 2Gy vs. 8Gy x 1-5 fractions). High-parameter flow cytometry and multiplex cytokine analysis will quantify changes in stromal cell phenotypes (CAFs, TAMs), endothelial activation markers, and immunogenic cell death signals (calreticulin, HMGB1, ATP). The optimal regimen will be defined by its ability to enhance the tumoricidal activity of co-cultured, patient-derived neoantigen-reactive T cell receptors (TCRs) identified via our cell-surface marker capture method.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel, biologically rational radiotherapy fractionation paradigm designed to condition the TME for cellular therapy. It will provide a pre-clinical framework for combining radiotherapy with adoptive T cell transfer, moving beyond empirical combinations. The data will yield specific, testable fractionation schedules for clinical translation in patients with defined neoantigen targets.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in using fractionation as a precise tool to co-target multiple TME compartments based on their differential radiation biology, with the explicit goal of amplifying a personalized neoantigen-specific T cell response, rather than maximizing direct cytoreduction.

proposals.title: NOS1 Activity as a Predictive Biosensor and Targetable Regulator of Neoantigen-Reactive TIL Efficacy in Metastatic Cancers
proposals.motivation: Adoptive cell therapy (ACT) with tumor-infiltrating lymphocytes (TILs) targeting patient-specific neoantigens shows promise, but response rates remain inconsistent. The tumor microenvironment (TME) is a critical but poorly understood determinant of TIL engraftment and function. Pre-2026 evidence implicates nitric oxide (NO) in immune modulation, yet its precise, context-dependent role in shaping the immunological landscape for ACT is unknown, representing a major barrier to predictable clinical success.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that spatial and temporal patterns of NOS1 expression in the TME create distinct immunological niches that dictate the functional fate of adoptively transferred, neoantigen-reactive T cells, and that pharmacologically modulating this axis can reprogram innate-adaptive crosstalk to enhance TIL therapy.
proposals.method: We will leverage our established platform using patient-derived tumor organoids and autologous TILs from metastatic epithelial cancers. Spatial transcriptomics (Visium) and multiplexed ion beam imaging (MIBI) will map NOS1 expression relative to immune subsets in pre-ACT biopsies. Organoid/TIL co-cultures will be treated with selective NOS1 inhibitors (e.g., Nω-Propyl-L-arginine) or NO donors to functionally test impact on neoantigen-reactive TCR activation (via CD137/OX40 assays) and NK cell cytotoxicity. Predictive models will be built using a Random Forest classifier integrating NOS1 spatial metrics with TIL transcriptomic signatures to forecast in vitro TIL efficacy.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish NOS1 activity as a novel, measurable biosensor of TME receptivity to TIL therapy. It will provide a mechanistic framework for how NO gradients differentially regulate immune subsets, moving beyond a blanket immunosuppressive view. Ultimately, it will deliver a rationale for NOS1-targeted TME conditioning as a combinatorial strategy to improve ACT outcomes.
proposals.novelty: This proposal reframes NOS1 not as a uniform immunosuppressive target but as a dynamic, spatial regulator of specific immune circuits. The novelty lies in using NOS1 as a precision tool to predict and then rationally modulate the host TME to favor TIL success, making therapy efficacy a programmable feature of the environment.

proposals.title: NOS1 Amplification Status as a Biomarker to Guide the Timing of Surgery Following Neoadjuvant Radiotherapy to Minimize Metastatic Risk
proposals.motivation: The sequencing of radiotherapy (RT) and surgery is critical in cancer care, but RT can induce systemic immunosuppression that may promote metastatic seeding. Pre-2026 pan-cancer analyses have identified NOS1 amplification as a driver of immunosuppressive myeloid and T-cell landscapes. This creates an urgent need to determine if NOS1 status can predict which patients are most vulnerable to RT-induced immunosuppression, informing personalized surgical timing.
proposals.hypothesis: Tumors harboring NOS1 amplification will exhibit a more profound and prolonged systemic immunosuppressive response following radiotherapy, leading to a narrower optimal window for surgical resection to prevent metastatic progression compared to NOS1-wildtype tumors.
proposals.method: We will first analyze TCGA pan-cancer data to correlate NOS1 amplification with baseline and post-treatment (where available) immune gene expression signatures (e.g., T-cell exhaustion, myeloid-derived suppressor cell markers). We will then curate retrospective clinical cohorts of patients treated with neoadjuvant RT followed by surgery, stratifying by NOS1 status (assessed via FISH or NGS). Using multivariate Cox regression, we will model the association between NOS1 status, time from RT to surgery, and outcomes (disease-free survival, metastatic recurrence). Machine learning (LASSO-penalized regression) will integrate NOS1 status, tumor type, and immune parameters to develop a predictive model for optimal surgical timing.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish NOS1 as the first genomic biomarker to stratify patients for surgery timing based on intrinsic immunosuppressive biology. It will provide tumor-type-specific clinical guidelines to minimize the metastatic risk of RT. The resulting decision tool could be prospectively validated to personalize multimodal therapy sequencing.
proposals.novelty: While NOS1's role in immunosuppression is known, its application to optimize the *timing* of a standard intervention (surgery) post-RT is novel. This directly bridges genomic immune profiling with a actionable clinical decision in the treatment pathway.

proposals.title: A patient-specific ecological model of IL-2-induced TME dynamics predicts optimal radiotherapy timing in metastatic melanoma
proposals.motivation: Despite the potential of combining immunotherapy and radiotherapy, clinical outcomes remain inconsistent. This is partly because the tumor microenvironment (TME) is a dynamic ecosystem, and the timing of radiotherapy relative to immunomodulation is critical but poorly understood. Pre-2026, we lack predictive models that integrate patient-specific T cell receptor (TCR) and neoantigen data with ecological principles to schedule these therapies rationally.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that IL-2 administration induces predictable, patient-specific state transitions in the TME's ecological network, and that radiotherapy administered during a transient 'permissive' state characterized by a high ratio of neoantigen-reactive to immunosuppressive T cells will yield superior tumor control.
proposals.method: We will parameterize a Lotka-Volterra-type competition model using patient-derived data. Single-cell RNA-seq and TCR sequencing from metastatic melanoma digests (pre/post IL-2) will quantify populations: neoantigen-reactive CD8+ T cells (captured via surface markers like CD137), regulatory T cells, myeloid-derived suppressor cells, and tumor cells. IL-2's effects will be modeled as altered interaction coefficients and carrying capacities. The model will be implemented in R/Python using ordinary differential equation solvers, with state transitions identified via bifurcation analysis. Model-predicted optimal timings will be validated in patient-derived tumor organoids co-cultured with autologous T cells, assessing radiation-induced cell death.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a first-in-class computational framework that links ecological modeling of the TME to actionable radiotherapy scheduling. It will provide a mechanistic rationale for personalized combination therapy schedules, moving beyond empirical timing. The validated model could serve as a clinical decision-support tool to overcome radioresistance.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in applying formal ecological state-transition models to the problem of therapy scheduling, directly integrating Dr. Rosenberg's established methods for isolating patient-specific neoantigen-reactive T cells to parameterize and ground the simulations in real human immunobiology.

proposals.title: A Microfluidic Tumor-Immune Platform for Real-Time, Single-Cell Mapping of Neoantigen-Specific T Cell Responses to Fractionated Radiotherapy
proposals.motivation: Radiotherapy (RT) can modulate the tumor immune microenvironment, potentially enhancing neoantigen presentation and T cell infiltration, but the dynamic, cell-type-specific interactions remain poorly understood. Bulk analyses and endpoint assays fail to capture the heterogeneous, real-time responses of cancer and immune cells, limiting our ability to rationally combine RT with adoptive T cell therapies targeting patient-specific neoantigens.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that integrating patient-derived tumor organoids with autologous neoantigen-reactive T cell receptors (TCRs) in a spatially structured microfluidic chip will reveal distinct, time-resolved cellular trajectories and paracrine signaling events in response to clinically relevant RT fractionation, identifying critical windows for effective T cell activation and tumor cell killing.
proposals.method: We will establish patient-derived epithelial cancer organoids and expand neoantigen-reactive T cells identified via existing MHC multimer or co-culture assays. These components will be integrated into a polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS) microfluidic device with controlled oxygen gradients and perfusion. A miniaturized X-ray source will deliver precise, fractionated doses. Response will be monitored via live-cell imaging and by harvesting cells for single-cell RNA sequencing (10x Genomics) and cytometry by time of flight (CyTOF) at multiple timepoints post-RT. Data integration and trajectory analysis will be performed using Seurat and Monocle3.
proposals.contribution: This work will generate the first dynamic, single-cell atlas of tumor-immune ecosystem responses to fractionated RT, directly linking radiation parameters to neoantigen-specific T cell functionality. It will establish a high-throughput preclinical platform to test personalized RT-immunotherapy schedules, directly informing the design of clinical trials combining adoptive T cell transfer with radiotherapy.
proposals.novelty: While organoid and TCR cloning methods exist, their integration into a controlled, radiobiological microfluidic system for continuous, multi-modal single-cell analysis is novel. This moves beyond static co-cultures to model the spatial and temporal dynamics of treatment response within a patient-specific context.

proposals.title: Single-cell profiling of the neoantigen-reactive T-cell repertoire and immunosuppressive microenvironment in patient-derived tumor organoids during radiotherapy
proposals.motivation: Radiotherapy (RT) can modulate the tumor immune microenvironment (TME), potentially enhancing neoantigen presentation and T-cell reactivity, but can also induce immunosuppressive mechanisms. Pre-2026, bulk analyses obscure the dynamic, cell-type-specific changes critical for understanding RT's immunogenic effects. A high-resolution map of individual T-cell and stromal cell responses is needed to identify biomarkers of productive anti-tumor immunity versus resistance.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that radiotherapy induces a bifurcated response within the TME: it expands a subset of pre-existing, patient-specific neoantigen-reactive T-cell clones while concurrently activating specific immunosuppressive stromal populations, and that single-cell profiling can identify predictive signatures of clinical response.
proposals.method: We will establish patient-derived tumor organoids (PDTOs) from metastatic epithelial cancers, as in Rosenberg's prior work, and treat them with clinically relevant RT fractions. Using the cell surface marker-based capture method (CD8, CD137/OX40) to isolate neoantigen-reactive T cells, we will perform paired single-cell RNA and TCR sequencing (scRNA-seq + scTCR-seq) on the entire PDTO TME pre- and post-RT. Data will be integrated using Seurat v4 and analyzed with the Monocle3 algorithm for trajectory inference to map lineage states and clonal expansion. Cell-cell communication will be modeled with CellChat.
proposals.contribution: This work will generate the first high-resolution atlas of dynamic, patient-specific TME remodeling during RT at single-cell resolution. It will directly link neoantigen-reactive TCR clonal dynamics to the concurrent evolution of immunosuppressive pathways, providing mechanistic insights into RT-induced immunogenicity. The identified cellular and molecular signatures could guide rational combinations of RT with immunotherapy.
proposals.novelty: While Rosenberg's group has captured neoantigen-reactive TCRs from bulk tumor digests and used PDTOs for T-cell analysis, applying these tools at single-cell resolution during a dynamic perturbation like radiotherapy is novel. This approach uniquely enables the deconvolution of competing pro- and anti-immunogenic cell states arising from the same treatment within a personalized model.

proposals.title: Longitudinal Neoantigen-Reactive TCR Tracking and State-Transition Modeling for Adaptive T-Cell Therapy in Metastatic Cancers
proposals.motivation: Current adoptive T-cell therapies targeting patient-specific neoantigens, while promising, often fail due to tumor immune evasion and a dynamic, suppressive tumor microenvironment (TME). Pre-2026, methods exist to isolate neoantigen-reactive T-cell receptors (TCRs) from tumors, but treatment is typically a single intervention based on a static TME snapshot. A critical unmet need is a framework to model how the TME and the reactive T-cell repertoire co-evolve under therapy to enable dynamic treatment re-optimization.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that frequent longitudinal profiling of the TME and the neoantigen-reactive TCR repertoire, integrated into a mechanistic state-transition model, will enable the prediction of near-future TME states and identify optimal times for therapeutic intervention or switch to sustain anti-tumor immunity.
proposals.method: We will leverage Dr. Rosenberg's established methods to isolate neoantigen-reactive CD8+ T cells and their TCRs from serial, minimally invasive biopsies or blood samples of patients undergoing therapy. Longitudinal multi-omic profiles (TCR-seq, scRNA-seq, cytokines) will feed into a hybrid mechanistic-machine learning model. This model will use ordinary differential equations to capture known TME interactions (e.g., T-cell exhaustion, clone competition) and a recurrent neural network (RNN) to learn temporal patterns and predict state transitions. A reinforcement learning agent will then simulate therapy actions (e.g., TCR-T cell infusion, checkpoint blockade) to identify sequences that maximize a durable response metric.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel clinical-computational pipeline for adaptive cancer immunotherapy. It will provide the first proof-of-concept that dynamic TME/TCR tracking coupled with predictive modeling can guide pre-emptive therapy changes. The resulting state-transition model will be a foundational tool for understanding treatment failure and designing resilient therapeutic regimens.
proposals.novelty: While others model TME statically, this integrates Dr. Rosenberg's unique capability to track the *functional* neoantigen-reactive TCR repertoire over time into a predictive dynamical systems framework, creating a closed-loop, patient-specific treatment optimizer.

proposals.title: A Biomimetic Tumor-on-Chip Platform to Dissect Radiotherapy-Induced Immunomodulation and Engineer Patient-Specific Neoantigen-Reactive TILs
proposals.motivation: Radiotherapy (RT) can induce immunogenic cell death and alter the tumor microenvironment (TME), potentially enhancing T-cell infiltration. However, pre-2026 models lack the spatial and dynamic complexity to study how RT-modulated TME signals guide patient-derived Tumor-Infiltrating Lymphocyte (TIL) behavior in real-time. This gap hinders the rational design of combined RT and adoptive TIL therapy, particularly for targeting neoantigens in common epithelial cancers.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that integrating a patient-derived tumor organoid with an engineered 3D lymphoid niche in a microfluidic device will reveal that specific RT dose/fractionation schedules create chemokine and DAMP gradients that selectively promote the expansion and functional differentiation of neoantigen-reactive T-cell clones within the TIL product.
proposals.method: We will fabricate a three-chamber polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS) microfluidic device. The central chamber will host patient-derived tumor organoids embedded in a collagen-Matrigel matrix. Adjacent chambers will contain porous polycaprolactone (PCL) scaffolds functionalized with T-cell supportive factors (e.g., ICAM-1, CCL19/21) as engineered lymphoid niches. Autologous TILs, pre-screened for neoantigen reactivity via IFN-γ ELISpot using patient organoids, will be loaded into the scaffold. A miniaturized X-ray source will deliver precise RT doses (e.g., 2-8 Gy) to the tumor chamber. Real-time monitoring of TIL migration (via live-cell imaging) and cytokine/DAMP secretion (via integrated biosensors) will be correlated with endpoint analysis of TILs harvested from scaffolds, including TCR sequencing to track clonal expansion and cytotoxicity assays against organoids.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a first-of-its-kind unified preclinical platform to dynamically test RT-TIL combination therapy. It will provide mechanistic insights into how RT parameters shape the TME to foster productive TIL responses. The platform will yield a directly harvestable, expanded TIL product for functional validation, offering a novel pipeline to personalize combined modality treatment protocols.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the closed-loop integration of a radiobiological stimulus with a engineered lymphoid tissue in a microfluidic TME, enabling real-time analysis of RT-induced immunomodulation and the concurrent physical expansion of patient-specific TILs for downstream therapeutic use—a capability beyond static co-cultures or in vivo models.

proposals.title: Defining the Immunosuppressive Microenvironment's Impact on Neoantigen-Reactive T Cell Receptor Function in Patient-Derived Tumor Organoids
proposals.motivation: Adoptive T cell therapies using neoantigen-reactive T cell receptors (TCRs) have shown promise, but clinical responses remain inconsistent. A key pre-2026 knowledge gap is that current ex vivo methods for identifying reactive TCRs, like tumor digests or co-culture with autologous antigen-presenting cells, largely ignore the immunosuppressive signals of the native tumor microenvironment (TME). This may select TCRs that are functionally impaired upon reinfusion into the patient.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the native TME, particularly myeloid-derived suppressor cells (MDSCs) and regulatory T cells (Tregs), directly suppresses the cytotoxic function of neoantigen-reactive CD8+ T cells, and that TCRs selected in the absence of this pressure will have reduced efficacy in vivo.
proposals.method: We will use patient-derived tumor organoids (PDTOs) from metastatic epithelial cancers (e.g., colorectal, cholangiocarcinoma) as a scaffold to reconstitute a patient-specific TME by adding back autologous peripheral blood-derived MDSCs and Tregs, isolated via fluorescence-activated cell sorting (FACS) using pre-2026 surface markers (e.g., CD33+HLA-DRlow for MDSCs, CD4+CD25+CD127low for Tregs). Neoantigen-reactive CD8+ T cell clones, identified from tumor-infiltrating lymphocytes (TILs) via established MHC multimer or co-culture assays, will be co-cultured with PDTOs ± immunosuppressive populations. T cell function will be quantified via multiplexed cytokine secretion (Luminex), cytotoxicity (Incucyte live-cell imaging), and TCR signaling activity (phospho-flow cytometry).
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel, patient-specific ex vivo platform to evaluate TCR function under physiologically relevant immunosuppression. It will provide a mechanistic link between specific TME components and T cell dysfunction, offering a rationale for combining TCR therapy with TME-targeting agents. The results will guide a more predictive selection of TCRs for clinical use.
proposals.novelty: While PDTOs have been used to test T cell reactivity, systematically reconstructing the autologous immunosuppressive compartment within them to pressure-test identified neoantigen-reactive TCRs is a novel, patient-centric approach to therapy optimization.

proposals.title: Synergistic Targeting of Tumor and Microenvironment: Combining Neoantigen-Reactive T-Cell Therapy with FGFR/MAPK Pathway Inhibition in Patient-Derived Organoid Models
proposals.motivation: Despite advances in adoptive T-cell therapy targeting neoantigens, many solid tumors exhibit resistance due to an immunosuppressive microenvironment and oncogenic signaling that impairs T-cell function and persistence. Pre-2026 work, including from our lab, has identified potent neoantigen-reactive T-cell receptors (TCRs) and patient-specific targets like FGFR2 fusions, but clinical responses remain limited. Signal transduction inhibitors (STIs) targeting pathways such as FGFR/MAPK are clinically available but often yield transient tumor control.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that co-administration of FGFR or MEK/ERK pathway inhibitors will enhance the antitumor efficacy of autologous neoantigen-reactive T cells by remodeling the tumor microenvironment, increasing target antigen presentation, and improving T-cell infiltration and function.
proposals.method: We will utilize our established platform of patient-derived tumor organoids (PDTOs) from epithelial cancers (e.g., cholangiocarcinoma with FGFR2 fusions) as ex vivo avatars. PDTOs will be treated with FGFR or MEK inhibitors (e.g., pemigatinib, trametinib) prior to and during co-culture with autologous tumor-infiltrating lymphocytes (TILs) or TCR-transduced T cells specific for identified neoantigens (e.g., from our FGFR2 fusion work). Tumor cell killing will be quantified via live-cell imaging and cytokine release (IFN-γ, Granzyme B ELISA). We will analyze changes in the PDTO transcriptome (RNA-seq) and T-cell phenotype (flow cytometry for activation/exhaustion markers) to define mechanistic synergy.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a preclinical framework for rationally combining targeted therapies with personalized cellular immunotherapies. It will identify specific signaling pathways whose inhibition optimally synergizes with T-cell attack and deliver biomarkers (e.g., immunogenic modulation signatures) to guide patient selection for combination trials.
proposals.novelty: While STI and immunotherapy combinations are being explored clinically, this proposal uniquely leverages patient-specific neoantigen-reactive TCRs and autologous PDTOs to model personalized synergy ex vivo, moving beyond generic immune checkpoint blockade combinations to a precision medicine approach.

proposals.title: Defining the Multivalent Neoantigen Landscape Required for Effective T-cell Targeting of Heterogeneous Glioblastoma
proposals.motivation: Glioblastoma (GBM) is characterized by profound intratumoral heterogeneity and co-existing dysregulated pathways, rendering single-target therapies ineffective. Pre-2026 work in Rosenberg's lab has successfully isolated neoantigen-reactive T-cell receptors (TCRs) from epithelial cancers, but the application of this powerful immunotherapeutic approach to GBM is hindered by the assumption that targeting a single neoantigen is sufficient. This project addresses the critical need to define the combinatorial neoantigen signatures required for robust anti-tumor immunity in heterogeneous brain tumors.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that effective T-cell-mediated killing of heterogeneous GBM requires the simultaneous targeting of a personalized, multi-pathway neoantigen repertoire, and that single neoantigen-reactive TCRs will be insufficient to control tumor growth due to antigen-negative escape variants.
proposals.method: We will leverage patient-derived GBM organoids and autologous tumor-infiltrating lymphocytes (TILs) from metastatic digests, as established in recent lab papers. Using the lab's cell surface marker-based capture technology (CD137/OX40), we will isolate T-cell clones reactive to predicted neoantigens from multiple core GBM pathways (e.g., RTK, p53, RB). We will then employ high-throughput combinatorial cytotoxicity assays in organoid co-cultures, systematically testing single TCRs versus defined TCR pools against the original and spatially distinct organoid sub-regions to map escape and efficacy.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first functional blueprint for multi-antigen T-cell therapy in GBM, moving the field beyond single-target approaches. It will establish a novel experimental framework for defining the minimal neoantigen repertoire needed to overcome heterogeneity, directly informing the design of effective, personalized TCR-based therapies for brain cancer patients.
proposals.novelty: While neoantigen-reactive TCRs have been identified, no pre-2026 study has systematically defined the combinatorial antigen requirements to preemptively block escape in a highly heterogeneous solid tumor using patient-specific organoid and TIL models.

ideas.title: NOS1-Informed Biomaterial Scaffolds for Patient-Specific TIL Rescue and Expansion
ideas.description: This project integrates the pan-cancer atlas of NOS1-mediated immune suppression with regenerative medicine scaffold technology to develop a new class of precision TIL expansion platforms. Biomaterial scaffolds will be engineered to incorporate spatial and biochemical cues—such as localized NOS1 inhibitors, metabolic modulators, and specific cytokine gradients—informed by the tumor-specific immune dysfunction signatures identified in the atlas. This allows for the creation of patient-tailored ex vivo microenvironments designed to counteract the exact immunosuppressive mechanisms (e.g., T-cell exhaustion, dysfunction) present in the donor tumor. Consequently, the approach enables the effective expansion of functional tumor-infiltrating lymphocytes from a broader range of cancer types, particularly those with high NOS1 amplification and historically poor TIL yields, translating a systemic biomarker into a direct therapeutic manufacturing strategy.

ideas.title: TME as a Catalytic Scaffold for Immunotherapy
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that the TME is primarily an obstacle by drawing an analogy from materials science, specifically heterogeneous catalysis. In that field, an inert support material is engineered to become an active catalytic component, enhancing the reaction. The analogous solution involves modifying the support's properties to stabilize and improve the performance of the active catalyst. Adapted to cancer immunotherapy, this means re-engineering the physical and biochemical properties of the TME—its extracellular matrix, stromal cells, and vasculature—to function not as a barrier but as a 'catalytic scaffold.' This scaffold would be designed with synthetic immunomodulatory cues to actively recruit, activate, and correctly position immune effector cells, thereby amplifying the local immune response and transforming the TME into a therapeutic amplifier.

ideas.title: TME-optimized radiotherapy fractionation to co-target tumor and stromal compartments for durable response
ideas.description: This research direction integrates the principle that cancer cell targeting is insufficient with the goal of optimizing radiotherapy fractionation. It proposes to systematically design and test dose-per-fraction schedules based on a quantitative understanding of how different doses affect not just tumor cells, but critical stromal, immune, and vascular components. The core hypothesis is that regimens exist which can simultaneously induce immunogenic tumor cell death, favorably reprogram immunosuppressive stromal cells, and preserve pro-inflammatory endothelial function, thereby converting the TME from a barrier into a therapeutic asset. The novel synergy lies in using fractionation as a precise tool to manipulate the multi-cellular system of the TME, aiming for regimens that are sub-optimal for killing isolated cancer cells but optimal for achieving durable systemic anti-tumor control.

ideas.title: In vitro specificity does not predict in vivo safety
ideas.description: Strong in vitro tumor-specific reactivity failed to predict fatal on-target off-tumor toxicity against normal lung epithelium.

ideas.title: NOS1-Modulated TME State Dictates TIL Efficacy by Reprogramming Innate-Adaptive Crosstalk
ideas.description: This research challenges the intrinsic view of TIL potency by proposing that the pre-existing tumor microenvironment (TME) state, specifically its spatial and temporal NOS1 (nitric oxide synthase 1) expression profile, is a dominant regulator of adoptive cell therapy outcome. It posits that NOS1-derived nitric oxide is not uniformly immunosuppressive but creates distinct TME niches that differentially activate or suppress specific immune subsets, such as enhancing NK cell function while potentially inhibiting certain T cell populations. The novel synergy lies in using NOS1 activity as a dynamic, measurable biosensor of the TME state to predict and then rationally modulate—through pharmacological inhibition or targeted delivery—the immunological context to favor TIL engraftment and cytotoxicity. This transforms NOS1 from a broad target into a precision tool for conditioning the TME, making TIL efficacy a programmable feature of the host environment rather than solely a property of the transferred cells.

ideas.title: Scale down to CAR-NK for reduced cytokine storm
ideas.description: Use natural killer cells instead of T cells as CAR carriers, producing a less inflammatory cytokine profile upon activation.

ideas.title: Questioning Tumor-Centric Ignoring Microenvironment Effects

ideas.title: Mechanistic Dissection of NOS1 as a Causal Immune Evasion Hub Across Cancers
ideas.description: This project moves beyond correlation to establish causal mechanisms of NOS1-mediated immune suppression. It begins with a pan-cancer atlas linking NOS1 amplification to multi-omic immune dysfunction signatures across TCGA, then validates findings using single-cell profiling of primary tumors to map NOS1 expression to specific cellular compartments. The core of the approach employs controlled perturbation experiments in genetically engineered mouse models and patient-derived organoid/immune cell co-cultures to test whether tumor- or myeloid-specific NOS1 overexpression is sufficient to induce T-cell dysfunction and resistance to immunotherapy. The final output is a validated, mechanistic model of NOS1 as an actionable immune evasion node.

ideas.title: Vaccines may need destruction not stimulation

ideas.title: TME-on-Chip with Integrated Biomaterial Scaffolds for Dynamic TIL-Radiotherapy Testing
ideas.description: This research integrates a microfluidic tumor-on-chip, designed to replicate the spatial and biomechanical features of the tumor microenvironment (TME), with porous, 3D biomaterial scaffolds seeded in adjacent chambers to serve as engineered lymphoid niches. The platform enables the precise delivery of radiotherapy to the tumor region while simultaneously monitoring how radiation-altered TME signals (e.g., chemokines, DAMPs) guide the infiltration, expansion, and functional differentiation of patient-derived Tumor-Infiltrating Lymphocytes (TILs) within the scaffolds. This closed-loop system allows for the real-time analysis of radiotherapy-induced immunogenic changes and the direct harvest of expanded TILs from the scaffolds for downstream potency assays. Consequently, it provides a unified preclinical model to optimize combined radiotherapy and TIL therapy protocols by testing how radiation dose and fractionation schedule shape the quality and quantity of the resulting TIL product.

ideas.title: Preventive vaccines versus therapeutic cancer vaccines

ideas.title: Spatially-Resolved Quantification of Radiotherapy-Induced Tumor Microenvironment Remodeling and Metastatic Seeding
ideas.description: This research program tests the hypothesis that radiotherapy (RT) creates specific, localized alterations in the tumor microenvironment (TME) that actively enhance the intravasation and survival of circulating tumor cells. The core innovation is a spatially fractionated RT approach in genetically engineered mouse models, which allows direct comparison of metastatic output from irradiated versus shielded sectors of the same primary tumor. By integrating longitudinal intravital imaging, single-cell RNA sequencing of the vascular niche, and in vivo fate mapping of disseminated cells, the study aims to identify the causal chain from RT-induced TME damage (e.g., vascular leakiness, fibroblast activation, immune exclusion) to increased metastatic efficiency, moving beyond correlation to mechanism.

ideas.title: Synthetic biology circuits for conditional T cell activation
ideas.description: Import synthetic biology logic gates to create T cells that activate only within the tumor microenvironment.
ideas.sourceTitle: Adoptive cell transfer as personalized immunotherapy for human cancer

ideas.title: NOS1-amplification-stratified surgical timing to counteract RT-induced systemic immunosuppression
ideas.description: This research integrates the pan-cancer NOS1 immunosuppression atlas with the concept of surgery timing optimization. The core hypothesis is that tumors with NOS1 amplification, which drives systemic immune suppression, will have a distinct and likely narrower optimal window between radiotherapy and surgery. The study will analyze TCGA and clinical cohorts to correlate NOS1 status with post-radiotherapy immune parameters and metastatic outcomes, aiming to define tumor-type-specific timing guidelines. The goal is to develop a clinical decision tool that uses NOS1 amplification status to schedule surgery at a time that minimizes the risk of RT-exacerbated immunosuppression and subsequent metastatic seeding.

ideas.title: Adaptive TME State-Transition Modeling for Dynamic Therapy Optimization
ideas.description: This system moves beyond a static baseline prediction by modeling the tumor microenvironment (TME) as a dynamical system that evolves under therapeutic pressure. It uses frequent, minimally invasive sampling to track TME components (e.g., immune cell populations, stromal factors, tumor clones) over time. These longitudinal profiles feed into a mechanistic model that predicts near-future TME states and identifies the therapy combination most likely to drive the system toward a durable response or susceptible state, enabling pre-emptive therapy switches. The approach closes the loop between prediction, intervention, and real-world TME adaptation, creating a continuously optimized treatment trajectory.

ideas.title: Predictive ecological modeling of IL-2-induced TME state transitions to optimize radiotherapy timing in metastatic melanoma
ideas.description: This research integrates ecological community dynamics models, such as Lotka-Volterra competition equations and state-transition models, with the specific immunomodulatory effects of IL-2 in the metastatic melanoma TME. The goal is to computationally simulate and predict how IL-2 administration alters the competitive and cooperative interactions between tumor cells, various T cell subsets, immunosuppressive populations, and stromal components over time. By linking these predicted ecological state transitions to experimentally measured changes in tumor radiosensitivity, the model will identify optimal therapeutic windows for radiotherapy administration post-IL-2. This approach moves beyond evaluating a static combination to dynamically scheduling interventions based on predicted, patient-specific TME evolution, aiming to overcome radioresistance more reliably.

ideas.title: Single-cell resolved tumor-on-chip platform for real-time TME-radiotherapy interaction mapping
ideas.description: This fused approach integrates single-cell multi-omics profiling (scRNA-seq, CyTOF, or spatial transcriptomics) directly with microfluidic tumor-on-chip platforms that recapitulate the tumor microenvironment architecture, enabling real-time monitoring of how each cell type—cancer cells, CAFs, TAMs, T cells, endothelial cells—responds to precisely dosed radiotherapy over time. The microfluidic chip provides exact spatial positioning and temporal control of radiation delivery, fluid flow, and oxygen gradients, while single-cell readouts capture the heterogeneous transcriptomic and phenotypic shifts that bulk or endpoint analyses would miss. This synergy unlocks the ability to construct dynamic cell-state trajectory maps of radiotherapy response within an intact TME context, identifying rare resistant subpopulations, paracrine signaling cascades, and immune activation kinetics at unprecedented resolution. The platform would serve as a high-throughput preclinical screening tool for optimizing RT dose fractionation schedules and combination immunotherapy strategies tailored to patient-derived tumor compositions.

ideas.title: Spatially-resolved single-cell profiling of TIL dysfunction in hypoxic niches suppressed by NOS1
ideas.description: This project integrates tumor hypoxia mapping (e.g., via FMISO-PET or MRI) with single-cell cytometry and transcriptomics of TILs extracted from spatially annotated regions. The core hypothesis is that tumor-derived NOS1 signaling is spatially heterogeneous and amplified within hypoxic regions, creating localized 'immune deserts' by selectively suppressing the interferon response and metabolic fitness of specific T-cell subsets. By correlating high-resolution hypoxia maps with single-cell functional data from matched geographic sectors, the study will identify the precise immunosuppressive mechanisms active in the most therapeutically resistant tumor regions. This enables the rational design of spatially-informed combination therapies, such as targeting NOS1 in hypoxic zones to precondition the TME for successful TIL or adoptive cell therapy.

ideas.title: Pharmacokinetic modeling from biologics to CAR-T
ideas.description: Adapting population pharmacokinetic modeling used for monoclonal antibodies to characterize CAR-T cell expansion kinetics.
ideas.sourceTitle: T cells expressing CD19 chimeric antigen receptors for acute lymphoblastic leukaemia in children and young adults: a phase 1 dose-escalation trial.

ideas.title: Vascularized, Immunocompetent Tumor-Stroma Assemblioid-on-Chip for Radiotherapy-TME Dynamics
ideas.description: This system constructs a physiologically relevant TME by co-culturing patient-derived tumor organoids with primary cancer-associated fibroblasts, endothelial cells, and immune cells in a 3D extracellular matrix within a multi-compartment microfluidic device. It features a perfusable, self-assembled microvascular network that enables immune cell extravasation and establishes biomimetic oxygen and nutrient gradients. The platform allows for precise, spatially targeted delivery of radiotherapy beams or radiomimetic drugs while monitoring real-time changes in cell viability, DNA damage, immune cell phenotype, cytokine secretion, and stromal remodeling. This integrated approach directly tests how the complex, cellularly heterogeneous TME modulates radiotherapeutic efficacy and drives the emergence of resistance.

ideas.title: A Modular TME-on-a-Chip Platform to Rank Stromal Contributions to Therapy Failure and Design Tumor-Specific Combination Regimens
ideas.description: This project addresses the generic nature of 'TME matters' critiques by building a discovery engine to identify which stromal elements are functionally critical in specific contexts. We will engineer a modular microfluidic co-culture system where a patient-derived tumor organoid is sequentially or combinatorially coupled with defined, isolated primary TME cell types (CAFs, TAMs, endothelial cells, etc.). This platform will be used to perform high-throughput screens of standard-of-care and targeted therapies, quantifying how each TME component modulates drug sensitivity. The output is a functional resistance map for each tumor, directly informing the design of prioritized, tumor-specific stromal-targeting combination therapies to overcome resistance.

ideas.title: TIL Efficacy as a Soil Fertility Problem: Pre-conditioning the TME
ideas.description: This idea challenges the core assumption that TIL therapeutic potency is an intrinsic property of the lymphocytes themselves. Drawing an analogy from agriculture and soil science, where a seed's potential is dominated by the pre-existing soil condition, we posit that pre-existing TME states may dominate therapeutic outcomes over TIL phenotype. The analogous solution of pre-conditioning soil before planting leads to an adapted research idea: we must first define the 'fertile' versus 'infertile' states of the TME for TIL engraftment and function, and then develop targeted 'pre-conditioning' therapies (e.g., modulating metabolism, depleting specific stromal elements, or transiently disrupting immunosuppressive networks) to be administered in a defined window *before* TIL infusion. This approach fundamentally shifts the strategy from intrinsic TIL optimization to extrinsic TME preparation, potentially unlocking the efficacy of existing TIL products.

ideas.title: Adapting Ecosystem Management Strategies to Model TME Intervention
ideas.description: This idea adapts solution strategies from conservation ecology and ecosystem management to model Tumor Microenvironment (TME) dynamics. In conservation, dynamic models of food webs and species interactions are used to forecast how targeted interventions reshape entire ecological communities. Analogously, we propose to model the TME as a complex ecological community where therapeutic interventions (e.g., immunotherapy, targeted drugs) are treated as perturbations. By applying frameworks that account for trophic cascades, competitive release, and indirect effects between cellular 'species' (e.g., various cancer cell subclones, T cells, macrophages, fibroblasts), the model would predict the secondary and long-term consequences of therapy, such as the emergence of resistant clones or the reprogramming of immune populations, ultimately forecasting whether an intervention will push the system toward a desirable stable state (effective tumor control) or an undesirable one (therapy escape).

ideas.title: Temporally programmable hydrogel niches for phenotype-preserving TIL expansion with sequential antigen restimulation
ideas.description: This system uses injectable, dynamically crosslinked hyaluronic acid hydrogels embedded with PLGA microparticles engineered for staged release of IL-21, IL-7, and anti-4-1BB agonist over 14-day expansion cultures, mimicking physiological signal sequencing rather than continuous cytokine flooding. Autologous tumor-derived antigen-presenting microgels are spatially distributed within the scaffold to provide periodic TCR restimulation, selecting for tumor-reactive clonotypes while preventing terminal differentiation. Real-time monitoring of scaffold-integrated oxygen and pH microsensors enables closed-loop perfusion adjustments that prevent metabolic stress-induced exhaustion. This approach directly counters the central failure mode of static scaffolds by ensuring that expanded TILs retain stem-like memory phenotypes (TCF1+, CD39−) with demonstrated superior persistence and antitumor function in preclinical adoptive transfer models.

ideas.title: Causally deconfounded pan-cancer atlas of radiotherapy-tumor microenvironment interactions with prospective validation
ideas.description: This project constructs a pan-cancer atlas of radiotherapy-induced tumor microenvironment changes using a causally rigorous framework that overcomes the confounding inherent in heterogeneous multi-cancer datasets. The approach mandates paired pre/post-RT biopsies from the same patients, applies causal inference methods (propensity score matching, sensitivity analyses for unmeasured confounders) to isolate RT-specific effects from platform, demographic, and treatment-schedule variation, and enforces standardized metadata inclusion criteria across all contributing datasets. Computationally identified RT-TME interaction signatures are then prospectively validated in syngeneic mouse models spanning at least five tumor types under identical fractionation protocols, establishing mechanistic causality. This design transforms a descriptive catalog into an actionable, causally grounded resource that can reliably inform RT-immunotherapy combination strategies across cancer types.

ideas.title: Prevent metastatic spreading via surgery timing using ecological succession principles
ideas.description: This idea expands the original concept by applying an analogy from ecology. In restoration ecology, a cleared site is vulnerable to invasion by undesirable pioneer species unless desired species are introduced at the right time to fill the niche and stabilize the community. Similarly, radiotherapy disrupts the tumor microenvironment (TME), creating a temporary 'cleared' state that may paradoxically facilitate the seeding of circulating tumor cells. The analogous solution is to manage this 'succession' within the TME. The adapted research idea is to systematically map the post-radiotherapy cellular and molecular 'successional timeline' in the TME to identify a therapeutic window. The goal is to time surgical resection when the repopulating niche is dominated by anti-metastatic, immune-activating, and tissue-stabilizing components, thereby using the body's own recovery dynamics to outcompete and prevent the establishment of metastatic deposits.

ideas.title: Sequencing TIL therapy after radiotherapy using crop rotation timing principles from agricultural science
ideas.description: This idea draws an analogy between optimizing TIL therapy timing after radiotherapy and the well-studied problem of plant-back interval optimization in agricultural science. In agriculture, the disturbance of cover crop termination triggers a transient restructuring of the soil microenvironment — beneficial microbes proliferate, nutrient cycling accelerates, and pathogen suppression peaks within a narrow temporal window. Farmers who plant cash crops within this window see significantly better yields and disease resistance. Similarly, radiotherapy creates a transient 'disturbance' in the tumor microenvironment: immunogenic cell death releases tumor antigens, pro-inflammatory cytokines surge, regulatory T cells and myeloid-derived suppressor cells temporarily decline, and vascular permeability increases. However, these favorable changes are transient — the immunosuppressive TME eventually reconstitutes. The proposed research would conduct systematic temporal profiling of key TME parameters (CD8+ T cell infiltration, MDSC frequency, IFN-gamma and CXCL9/10 levels, PD-L1 expression, vascular permeability via DCE-MRI) at 24-hour intervals from 1 to 14 days post-RT in preclinical tumor models. These kinetic profiles would be used to construct a 'TME receptivity index' — a composite score analogous to the soil readiness metrics used in agriculture — that predicts optimal TIL infusion timing. The approach would be validated by infusing TILs at the predicted optimal window versus earlier and later timepoints, measuring engraftment efficiency, expansion, and tumor control. This agricultural-inspired framework replaces empirical trial-and-error with a principled, data-driven timing optimization strategy that could be personalized using minimally invasive liquid biomarkers reflecting TME dynamics in individual patients.

ideas.title: Spatially resolved single-cell TME atlas of radiotherapy response using multiplexed in situ profiling
ideas.description: This study constructs a spatially resolved single-cell atlas of the tumor microenvironment during radiotherapy response by combining multiplexed spatial transcriptomics (MERFISH or CosMx) with multiplexed protein imaging (CODEX) on serial tissue sections from matched pre- and post-RT biopsies. By profiling cells in situ, the approach preserves critical spatial relationships—such as immune cell infiltration gradients, perivascular niches, and tumor-immune interfaces—that dissociation-based methods destroy, while simultaneously avoiding enzymatic dissociation artifacts that confound RT-response gene signatures. A parallel single-nucleus RNA-seq arm on adjacent tissue provides unbiased transcriptome-wide discovery, with computational integration mapping nuclear transcriptomes onto spatial coordinates to achieve both breadth and spatial context. This design enables identification of spatially defined cellular neighborhoods that predict immunogenic versus immunosuppressive RT responses, yielding actionable biomarkers for combination immunotherapy-RT strategies.

ideas.title: Pan-Cancer Atlas of Radiotherapy-Modulated Hypoxic Niches to Guide Spatially-Informed TIL Therapy
ideas.description: This research program integrates advanced hypoxia imaging with a systematic pan-cancer atlas of radiotherapy-induced TME changes to develop a spatiotemporal guide for TIL therapy. It moves beyond using static hypoxia maps to avoid hostile regions, instead actively mapping how radiotherapy dynamically alters hypoxic niches across different cancer types, creating transient 'windows of opportunity' for TIL infiltration and survival. The core synergy is generating a predictive model that informs both the optimal anatomical location and the optimal post-radiotherapy timing for TIL delivery, tailored to the specific cancer's TME response pattern. This enables a novel therapeutic strategy: using a defined radiotherapy dose to 'pre-condition' the tumor microenvironment, making it more receptive to subsequently administered TILs.

ideas.title: Adapting Microbial Community Perturbation Mapping to Identify NOS1 Pathway Network Dependencies
ideas.description: Drawing an analogy from microbial ecology, where genetic perturbations in synthetic communities are used to map functional dependencies and keystone species mediating community phenotypes, this idea adapts that solution to the tumor-immune microenvironment. The analogous solution involves high-throughput perturbation of defined community members to see how loss-of-function alters community-wide output. Adapted here, we propose constructing a focused CRISPR knockout library in melanoma cells targeting signaling, metabolic, and epigenetic regulators hypothesized to interact with the NOS1 pathway. These genetically perturbed melanoma pools will be co-cultured in parallel with standardized immune cells (e.g., PBMCs or specific T cell subsets). The key adaptation is using single-cell RNA sequencing of the immune cells as the multi-dimensional readout, rather than just melanoma cell fitness. This allows for systematic mapping of how each melanoma gene knockout alters the immune cell transcriptional landscape, specifically identifying which perturbations reverse the NOS1-driven interferon suppression signature. This approach moves beyond identifying mediator genes to mapping the functional network of dependencies surrounding NOS1 in the context of the tumor-immune interface.

ideas.title: Adapting the 'Master Regulator' Fallacy from Oncology to Chromosome 12q22-24 Amplification
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that NOS1 is the primary driver of IFN suppression in contexts of 12q22-24 amplification by drawing an analogy from cancer genomics. In oncology, the initial focus on a prominent gene within an amplicon (the 'master regulator' fallacy) often gave way to the understanding that co-amplified genes within the same chromosomal region can be critical functional drivers or modulators. The analogous solution involved systematically dissecting all genes in the amplicon using functional genomics. Adapted to this context, the research idea is to employ a high-throughput functional screen (e.g., CRISPRi) targeting all genes in the 12q22-24 amplicon in a model system with active IFN signaling. This unbiased approach will map the genetic landscape of IFN suppression, identifying which amplified gene(s) are essential for the phenotype. This could reveal that suppression is mediated by other genes in the region independently of NOS1, requires NOS1 plus specific co-amplified modifiers, or is driven by a completely different amplified effector, thereby refining the therapeutic target hypothesis.

ideas.title: Prevent metastatic spreading via adaptive, biomarker-guided surgery timing
ideas.description: This research develops a closed-loop clinical decision system to personalize the radiotherapy-to-surgery interval based on real-time assessment of metastatic risk. It employs serial liquid biopsies and advanced imaging to quantify dynamic, patient-specific signals of TME disruption, immune activation, and systemic tumor cell dissemination following radiotherapy. A validated predictive algorithm integrates these multi-omic biomarkers to identify the optimal surgical window for each patient—when anti-tumor immunity is maximized and pro-metastatic pressure is minimized—thereby moving beyond a single population-derived interval to an adaptive, biology-driven intervention.

ideas.title: Pulsed Dosing Schedules for TME Optimization Inspired by Antimicrobial Pharmacokinetic-Pharmacodynamic Modeling
ideas.description: This idea draws an analogy between optimizing radiotherapy dose fractionation for tumor microenvironment remodeling and the well-established PK/PD dose-fractionation framework used in antimicrobial pharmacology. In infectious disease, systematically varying drug dose, interval, and duration revealed that optimal bacterial killing and resistance suppression depend on whether the drug's efficacy is time-dependent or concentration-dependent, and that a 'mutant selection window' exists where suboptimal concentrations actively promote resistance. Adapting this framework to radiation oncology, we propose defining a 'radioresistance selection window' — specific dose-per-fraction ranges that enrich for radioresistant tumor clones or drive immunosuppressive TME polarization (e.g., M2 macrophage accumulation, regulatory T-cell expansion, or TGF-beta upregulation). The research program would: (1) construct mathematical models analogous to PK/PD models that integrate radiation dose-per-fraction with dynamic TME readouts (immune cell infiltration, cytokine profiles, hypoxia markers) over time; (2) perform systematic in vivo dose-fractionation studies in syngeneic tumor models, varying dose per fraction (0.5–12 Gy) and inter-fraction intervals while profiling TME composition via flow cytometry and spatial transcriptomics; (3) classify TME remodeling responses as 'time-dependent' (favoring prolonged low-dose exposure, akin to metronomic scheduling) or 'concentration-dependent' (favoring fewer, larger fractions with rest intervals for immune recovery); and (4) identify optimal fractionation windows that maximize favorable TME remodeling — such as dendritic cell activation, CD8+ T-cell priming, and vascular normalization — while avoiding the radioresistance selection window. This cross-disciplinary approach replaces empirical fractionation testing with a principled, model-driven framework that could accelerate identification of immunologically optimal radiotherapy regimens.

ideas.title: NOS1 inhibition to restore IFN signaling via a metabolic 'brake release' mechanism
ideas.description: This idea uses an analogy from plant physiology, where inhibitors of constitutive ethylene biosynthesis (e.g., AVG) are used to restore sensitivity to beneficial ethylene signaling pulses. The parallel problem in immunology is that chronic nitric oxide (NO) production from NOS1 in the tumor microenvironment may desensitize immune cells to IFN-α. The adapted solution is to test pharmacological NOS1 inhibitors not merely as direct anti-tumor agents, but as a means to reduce the desensitizing 'NO noise,' thereby rescuing IFN-α responsiveness in PBMCs and enhancing adoptive T cell therapy outcomes. This approach frames NOS1 inhibition as a metabolic 'brake release' to restore signaling circuit fidelity, analogous to restoring ethylene sensitivity in plants.

ideas.title: RT-Induced Metastatic Permeability: A Civil Engineering Analogy for TME Stabilization
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that radiotherapy's effects are solely tumoricidal by proposing it may inadvertently promote metastasis by disrupting the protective architecture of the tumor microenvironment (TME), akin to how excavation or seismic activity destabilizes a slope. Drawing an analogy from civil engineering, where solutions like retaining walls and grouting are used to proactively stabilize compromised slopes and prevent landslides, this research adapts that solution. It proposes to investigate combination therapies where RT is paired with agents designed to actively 'reinforce' the TME—such as stromal-modulating or extracellular matrix-stabilizing drugs—to test if this can counteract architectural disruption, preserve the TME's containment function, and prevent the facilitation of metastatic escape.

ideas.title: NOS1-genotype-stratified biomaterial scaffolds for personalized TIL expansion
ideas.description: This fused approach first uses Mendelian randomization at the NOS1 locus to stratify patients by genetically predicted nitric oxide (NO) levels and their causal impact on T-cell suppression. Patients carrying NOS1 variants linked to elevated NO-mediated immune suppression are then matched to tissue-engineered scaffolds incorporating NO scavengers or iNOS inhibitors within the biomaterial matrix, creating microenvironments that neutralize the patient-specific suppressive signal during ex vivo TIL expansion. The MR-derived causal estimates guide scaffold formulation — tuning scavenger concentration proportional to the predicted NO burden — transforming a one-size-fits-all expansion protocol into a genotype-informed, precision manufacturing pipeline. This convergence yields both a validated causal biomarker for TIL expansion failure and an actionable biomaterial intervention, closing the loop between genetic risk prediction and engineered therapeutic correction.

ideas.title: Single-cell metabolic cartography of immune suppression by tumor NOS1
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from microbial ecology, where multi-omics is used to trace how a pollutant disrupts the metabolic network of a complex community. Here, tumor-derived NOS1 signaling is the disruptive agent. The adapted method employs integrated single-cell multi-omics (e.g., CITE-seq combined with a metabolic tracer for arginine/nitric oxide pathways) on PBMCs. This creates a high-resolution metabolic and transcriptional map to identify which immune cell subsets have their interferon response most biochemically suppressed, tracing the precise point of failure (e.g., arginine depletion, mitochondrial dysfunction, transcriptional blockade) within the IFN signaling cascade caused by the tumor's metabolic activity.

ideas.title: Inverse Problem: Predicting Etiology from Tumor

ideas.title: Multi-parametric Tumor Niche Mapping for Strategic TIL Delivery
ideas.description: This approach moves beyond simple hypoxia mapping to a comprehensive, image-based definition of the tumor microenvironment. By integrating perfusion, metabolic, and cellular density data, the system identifies 'permissive niches' that balance TIL access to nutrients and oxygen with proximity to target tumor regions. TILs are then delivered via image-guided injection into these calculated perivascular niches, from which they can expand and actively migrate into adjacent, including hypoxic, tumor territories. This strategy aims to maximize TIL survival, persistence, and cytotoxic function by avoiding direct placement into metabolically hostile zones.

ideas.title: Atlas-based pan-cancer TME-RT mapping via Earth systems reanalysis methodology
ideas.description: This idea draws an analogy between constructing a pan-cancer atlas of radiotherapy-induced tumor microenvironment changes and the climate science practice of building global reanalysis datasets. In climate science, reanalysis projects like ERA5 solved the problem of making sense of fragmented, multi-source, multi-scale observational data by developing rigorous data assimilation frameworks that harmonize all inputs into a single consistent gridded product spanning decades and the entire globe. The adapted approach treats each cancer type as a distinct 'climate zone' with its own baseline TME ecology, and radiotherapy as an external forcing perturbation analogous to volcanic eruptions or El Niño events. A standardized computational pipeline would ingest publicly available and newly generated multi-omic data from irradiated tumors across at least 15 cancer types, applying cross-platform batch correction (analogous to instrument bias correction in reanalysis), unified cell-type deconvolution, and hierarchical modeling to produce a queryable atlas. The atlas would enable users to ask questions like: which immune cell infiltration patterns are universally induced by RT across cancers (analogous to universal climate responses), and which are cancer-type-specific (analogous to regional climate phenomena)? Quality tiers would flag data density per cancer type, akin to observational coverage maps in climate products, guiding users on confidence levels. This framework transforms scattered single-cancer RT-TME studies into a systematic, reproducible, and extensible resource for the radiation oncology and immuno-oncology communities.
